class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Project Management and the PMBOK Framework
learning_objectives: Define a project and distinguish project work from ongoing operations
Explain the value of project management to organizations and describe the evolution of the discipline
Identify the PMBOK Guide's performance domains, principles, and project lifecycle phases
Describe the role and responsibilities of the project manager and the skills required for success
readings: PMI, A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), 7th ed., Sections 1-2 (Introduction and System for Value Delivery)
Kerzner, Project Management: A Systems Approach to Planning, Scheduling, and Controlling, 13th ed., Ch. 1-2
Schwalbe, Information Technology Project Management, 9th ed., Ch. 1
HBR, 'The Rise of the Project Economy' (Nieto-Rodriguez, 2021)

lecture_notes.introduction: Almost every significant thing an organization creates, a new product, a factory, a software system, a merger integration, a marketing campaign, begins as a project. A project is a temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result. The word 'temporary' distinguishes projects from operations: operations run the business, projects change the business. NASA's Apollo 11 program put a man on the moon in eight years with technology that did not exist when the goal was set. Boeing's 787 development cost tens of billions of dollars and years of delays. Denver International Airport's baggage handling system became a case study in how projects fail catastrophically when complexity is underestimated. Each of these was managed (or mismanaged) using the tools and frameworks you will learn in this course. Project management has evolved from the scheduling techniques used to build the Polaris missile in the 1950s into a comprehensive discipline governing billions of dollars of work worldwide. The Project Management Institute (PMI) publishes the PMBOK Guide, the most widely adopted framework for project management practice. Its seventh edition (2021) shifted from a process-based to a principles-based and performance-domain approach, recognizing that the best project managers adapt their approach to context rather than mechanically applying a fixed set of processes. This course teaches you to apply those principles to real projects: to write a charter that aligns stakeholders, to build a work breakdown structure that converts ambition into actionable tasks, to schedule and budget credibly, to identify and respond to risks before they become crises, and to close projects in a way that captures learning for the next one. By the end of the semester, you will be able to take a project from idea to closure and explain the choices you made along the way.
key_concepts: Project definition: temporary (definite start and end), unique (produces a distinct deliverable), and progressively elaborated (requirements and plans become more detailed over time)

Project vs program vs portfolio: a project delivers one outcome, a program coordinates related projects toward strategic benefits, and a portfolio aligns projects and programs with organizational strategy
The triple constraint (iron triangle): scope, schedule, and cost, each influencing quality; a change to one typically forces a change in at least one of the others
PMBOK 7 performance domains: stakeholders, team, development approach and lifecycle, planning, project work, delivery, measurement, and uncertainty, replacing the 49 processes of PMBOK 6

PMBOK 7 principles: stewardship, team, stakeholders, value, systems thinking, leadership, tailoring, quality, complexity, risk, adaptability and resiliency, and change, twelve principles that guide project behavior
Project lifecycle phases: initiation, planning, execution, monitoring and controlling, and closing; phases can be sequential (predictive/waterfall), iterative, incremental, or adaptive (agile)

Project manager responsibilities: integrating stakeholder needs, defining and managing scope, leading the team, communicating status, managing risks, and delivering value aligned with the business case
The PM talent triangle: technical project management, leadership, and strategic and business management; modern PMs must combine all three
Project success criteria: on time, on budget, in scope is traditional; modern success includes stakeholder satisfaction, business value realized, and team well-being
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the Apollo 11 program as a real example: define its scope (land a man on the moon and return him safely by end of decade), its schedule (1961-1969), its budget (approximately $25 billion, over $150 billion in today's dollars), its stakeholders (President, Congress, NASA, contractors, public), and its success criteria. Compare with a modern example: the rollout of Healthcare.gov in 2013, which failed because scope was ambiguous, schedule was rigid, and risks were not managed. Introduce students to PMI and the Project Management Professional (PMP) credential, and discuss how certification relates to career progression.
setup_requirements: Access to the PMBOK Guide 7th edition (library or PMI student membership)
Microsoft Project or GanttPRO trial accounts established
Team formation for semester project teams (4-5 students each)

review.value: Every project has a definite end. A project that runs indefinitely has become operations. Every project produces something unique; the first website a company builds is a project, the tenth website built from a template is likely operations or a repeatable delivery process

PMBOK 7 teaches principles to guide decisions rather than processes to follow mechanically; this reflects the reality that projects differ so greatly in size, industry, and complexity that no single process fits all. A good PM tailors approach to context

A project exists to deliver business value; the business case justifies the investment and defines the success criteria. If you cannot articulate why the project matters to the organization, you cannot make good trade-off decisions later

When a stakeholder asks for more scope at the same schedule and cost, the PM's job is to show the trade-off explicitly: more scope requires more time, more money, or reduced quality. The triple constraint is not a law of physics but a communication tool

class_schedule.topic: Project Initiation, Charter, and Stakeholder Management
learning_objectives: Develop a project charter that authorizes the project and defines high-level scope, objectives, and stakeholders
Identify project stakeholders and analyze their power, interest, and influence
Develop a stakeholder engagement plan tailored to stakeholder categories
Apply techniques to manage stakeholder expectations throughout the project lifecycle
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.1 (Stakeholder Performance Domain)
Kerzner, Ch. 5 - Management of Scope and Stakeholders
Schwalbe, Ch. 4 - Project Integration Management and Charter Development
Freeman, Strategic Management: A Stakeholder Approach (selected chapters)
HBR, 'Stakeholder Theory in Practice' (2020)

lecture_notes.introduction: Projects fail more often because of stakeholder problems than because of technical problems. Stakeholders are the people and groups who influence or are affected by the project: sponsors who fund it, users who will use it, teams who will build it, executives who will judge it, customers who will pay for it, regulators who will inspect it, and communities who may benefit or suffer from its outcomes. Identifying them and managing their expectations is often the most consequential work a project manager does. Before any real work begins, most organizations require a project charter, a short document that formally authorizes the project, names the project manager, assigns authority, and captures the initial scope, objectives, business case, and key stakeholders. A good charter is the PM's license to operate: it is what you point to when someone tries to add scope without process, or when a competing priority threatens to pull resources from the project. Getting the charter right saves weeks of confusion later. Stakeholder analysis takes the charter's rough list and turns it into actionable intelligence: who has power, who cares, who can help, who can hurt. The classic tool is the power/interest grid. Once stakeholders are mapped, the PM develops engagement strategies tailored to each group, keeping high-power, high-interest stakeholders closely engaged and managing expectations of those with less influence. The point is not to manipulate people; it is to give each stakeholder the attention and information they need to support (or at least not derail) the project.
key_concepts: Project charter contents: project purpose, measurable objectives, high-level requirements, high-level risks, summary schedule, budget summary, stakeholder list, sponsor authority, and project manager authority
Business case: justifies the project investment in terms of strategic fit, benefits, costs, risks, and alternatives considered; link to ROI, NPV, payback period
Stakeholder identification: internal stakeholders (sponsor, team, functional managers, executives) and external stakeholders (customers, users, suppliers, regulators, community, media)
Stakeholder analysis: power/interest grid classifies stakeholders into four quadrants (manage closely, keep satisfied, keep informed, monitor); salience model adds legitimacy and urgency dimensions
Stakeholder engagement levels (PMBOK): unaware, resistant, neutral, supportive, and leading; the engagement plan documents current and desired level for each stakeholder
Communication tailoring: different stakeholders need different cadence, channel, and depth; executives want executive summaries, team members want task detail, regulators want compliance evidence
Managing upward: communicating with sponsors and executives requires anticipating their questions, framing options rather than problems, and being honest about status
Managing resistance: resistant stakeholders are not adversaries but people whose concerns are not yet addressed; active listening, acknowledging concerns, and adjusting the plan often converts them
Stakeholder register: living document capturing name, role, contact, requirements, expectations, influence level, engagement level, and communication preferences for each identified stakeholder
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a real project charter from a university construction project: a new engineering building. Examine the purpose (meet enrollment growth), objectives (5,000 sq ft of labs, LEED Gold certification, complete by August 2027), high-level scope (demolish old structure, new four-story building, parking integration), exclusions (does not include landscaping of adjacent plaza), budget ceiling ($42M), sponsor (Provost), and PM authority (approve up to $250K changes without board review). Then build a power/interest grid identifying the regents, provost, engineering dean, faculty, students, construction firm, city permitting office, and neighborhood association, placing each in the appropriate quadrant with a tailored engagement strategy.
setup_requirements: Case study: Sydney Opera House stakeholder analysis

review.value: The charter is the formal agreement between the sponsor and the project manager about what will be done, by when, and for how much. A vague charter produces vague accountability; a precise charter provides a stable reference point when scope pressures arise

Stakeholder identification is not a one-time activity. New stakeholders emerge as the project progresses; existing stakeholders change role or interest. Revisiting the stakeholder register at each phase boundary catches these changes before they become surprises

The power/interest grid is a useful first cut, but real stakeholder dynamics are more nuanced. A stakeholder with low power today may have high power next quarter; a stakeholder with low interest may become highly engaged when the project affects their area

review.key: Communication is the PM's Primary Activity
review.value: Studies show project managers spend 80-90% of their time communicating. The choice is not whether to communicate but whether to communicate intentionally and effectively. Poor communication is the most common root cause of project failure in surveys

class_schedule.topic: Scope Management and the Work Breakdown Structure
learning_objectives: Define the difference between product scope and project scope
Decompose project scope into a work breakdown structure (WBS) following the 100% rule and the 8-80 rule
Write WBS dictionary entries that unambiguously define each work package
Apply techniques to prevent and manage scope creep
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.4 (Planning Performance Domain)
Kerzner, Ch. 11 - Work Breakdown Structure
Schwalbe, Ch. 5 - Project Scope Management
PMI Practice Standard for Work Breakdown Structures, 2nd ed.

lecture_notes.introduction: If the charter tells you why, the scope tells you what. Scope management is the set of activities that define what work is in the project, what is not, and how the two are distinguished. Product scope describes the features and functions of the deliverable: a web application's user stories, a building's architectural specifications, a training program's learning objectives. Project scope describes the work required to deliver the product: development tasks, construction activities, curriculum design. Both must be defined clearly, and both must be controlled. Scope creep, the gradual expansion of scope without corresponding adjustments to schedule, budget, or resources, is the single most common cause of project cost and schedule overruns. The primary tool for defining project scope is the work breakdown structure (WBS). A WBS is a hierarchical decomposition of the total scope into progressively smaller and more manageable pieces, ending in work packages small enough to estimate, assign, and track. The 100% rule states that the WBS must contain 100% of the work required by the project and only that work: no missing activities and no gold-plating. The 8-80 rule offers practical guidance for work package size: each should require between 8 and 80 hours of effort, small enough to manage and large enough not to drown in micromanagement overhead. A well-constructed WBS is the foundation for scheduling, estimating, resource planning, risk identification, and progress tracking: every activity downstream depends on it. Building a good WBS is a team sport; involving the people who will do the work produces both a better decomposition and better buy-in.
key_concepts: Product scope: what the product will do, its features and functions, captured in requirements specifications, user stories, or architectural drawings
Project scope: the work required to deliver the product, including deliverables, management activities, testing, training, and transition
Scope statement: a detailed description of project scope, deliverables, acceptance criteria, exclusions, constraints, and assumptions; approved by sponsor before scope baseline is set
Work Breakdown Structure (WBS): hierarchical decomposition into deliverables and work packages; shown as a tree or outline; deliverable-oriented not activity-oriented
100% rule: the WBS contains 100% of scope at every level; children of a parent node must sum to exactly the parent, with no missing work and no extras
8-80 rule: work packages should require 8-80 hours of effort; larger suggests decomposing further, smaller suggests aggregating
WBS dictionary: companion document giving each work package a unique code, description, responsible party, entry criteria, exit criteria, estimated effort, and associated resources
Scope baseline: the approved scope statement, WBS, and WBS dictionary; changes require formal change control
Scope creep: uncontrolled expansion of scope, often through informal stakeholder requests that are not routed through change control; the leading cause of project failure
Scope change control: the formal process for evaluating, approving, and integrating scope changes, including impact analysis on schedule and budget
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a WBS live for a student event: 'Host a two-day engineering career fair for 500 students and 30 companies.' Level 1: the project itself. Level 2: major deliverables, Venue, Companies, Students, Logistics, Communications, Evaluation. Level 3: decompose each, e.g., Venue becomes Site selection, Layout, Setup, Teardown; Companies becomes Recruitment, Registration, Booth assignment, Hospitality. Level 4: work packages, e.g., Site selection becomes Identify candidates, Tour venues, Negotiate contract, Sign agreement. Stop when work packages fit the 8-80 rule. Write a WBS dictionary entry for one work package showing the structure: code 3.2.1, description, owner, effort estimate 16 hours, entry and exit criteria. Discuss how this WBS becomes the input for scheduling: each work package becomes one or more schedule activities.
setup_requirements: Project scenario: construction of a retail bank branch

review.value: A WBS is organized around what you will produce (deliverables), not what you will do (activities). This orientation makes the 100% rule testable: every deliverable is either in the WBS or it is not. Activities come later, in scheduling, derived from the WBS

Gold plating is adding scope that was not requested, often with good intentions. It inflates schedule and budget without authorization and violates the 100% rule. A mature PM does only what the charter authorizes and defers 'nice to have' ideas to the next release

The best WBS is built collaboratively with subject matter experts and people who will do the work. They catch missing activities, challenge unrealistic groupings, and commit more strongly to a decomposition they helped create
Everything downstream, schedule, budget, resources, risk register, quality plan, depends on the WBS. Time spent getting the WBS right pays back many times in cleaner downstream planning and execution

class_schedule.topic: Schedule Management: Activity Definition, Sequencing, and Estimating
learning_objectives: Transform WBS work packages into a detailed activity list with dependencies
Identify the four types of logical dependencies (FS, SS, FF, SF) and apply leads and lags
Estimate activity durations using analogous, parametric, three-point, and expert judgment techniques
Construct an activity-on-node (AON) network diagram
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.4 (Planning) subsections on schedule
Kerzner, Ch. 12 - Network Scheduling Techniques
Schwalbe, Ch. 6 - Project Schedule Management
Goldratt, Critical Chain (selected chapters) for multi-project scheduling

lecture_notes.introduction: Schedule management turns the WBS into a plan for when work happens. It answers the stakeholder's most common question: when will this be done? Getting schedule right is hard because real work has dependencies (you cannot pour a foundation before excavating), resources are limited (only two licensed electricians on the team), estimates are uncertain (has anyone ever built exactly this before?), and reality is messier than any plan. The discipline of schedule management provides tools to build credible schedules, update them as work progresses, and communicate status honestly. The first step is decomposing work packages into activities, the verbs that will actually be performed. A work package 'Database schema design' becomes activities: gather requirements, draft schema, review with architects, revise, finalize. Activities are then sequenced based on dependencies. The most common type is finish-to-start (FS): activity B cannot start until activity A finishes. Other types exist: start-to-start (SS) for parallel work that must begin together, finish-to-finish (FF) for activities that must end together, and the rare start-to-finish (SF). Leads (activity B can start before A finishes by some amount) and lags (activity B must wait some additional time after A finishes) refine these relationships. Once sequenced, activities need duration estimates, and this is where project managers most often deceive themselves. Human beings are systematically optimistic about how long work takes, a cognitive bias called the planning fallacy. Techniques like three-point estimation force the estimator to consider pessimistic and optimistic scenarios, producing more realistic estimates. The output of this session is an activity network diagram showing the logical flow of work, which becomes the input for critical path analysis in the next session.
key_concepts: Activity definition: breaking work packages into specific schedule activities with distinct start and finish times
Precedence Diagramming Method (PDM): activity-on-node representation with four dependency types; activity-on-arrow is the older AOA method
Finish-to-Start (FS): most common; successor starts after predecessor finishes; e.g., pour foundation FS frame walls
Start-to-Start (SS): successor starts when predecessor starts; e.g., start writing code SS start writing tests
Finish-to-Finish (FF): successor finishes when predecessor finishes; e.g., testing FF development (testing cannot complete until development completes)
Start-to-Finish (SF): successor finishes only after predecessor starts; rare, used in shift handovers
Leads (negative lag): successor can start before predecessor finishes by a specified amount; e.g., writing user manual can start 80% through development
Lags: required delay between activities; e.g., pour concrete FS install drywall with 7-day lag for curing
Analogous estimating: uses similar past projects to estimate; fast but less accurate; good for early planning
Parametric estimating: uses statistical relationships (e.g., square footage times cost per square foot); accurate when parametric data exists
Three-point estimating: uses optimistic (O), most likely (M), and pessimistic (P); triangular mean = (O+M+P)/3; beta/PERT mean = (O+4M+P)/6; standard deviation = (P-O)/6
Bottom-up estimating: aggregates activity estimates from WBS; most accurate but time-consuming
Planning fallacy: the cognitive tendency to underestimate task duration; countered by three-point estimation and historical data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build an activity list and network for a website redesign project. Activities: requirements (5 days), wireframes (4 days), visual design (6 days), content writing (8 days), frontend development (15 days), backend development (12 days), integration (5 days), testing (7 days), deployment (2 days). Dependencies: requirements FS wireframes, wireframes FS visual design, wireframes FS content writing (parallel), visual design FS frontend, requirements FS backend (parallel to visual/frontend), frontend FS integration, backend FS integration, content writing FF frontend (content needed before frontend finishes), integration FS testing, testing FS deployment. Draw the AON network diagram. Apply three-point estimating to the frontend activity: O=10, M=15, P=25, PERT mean = (10+60+25)/6 = 15.8 days, standard deviation = (25-10)/6 = 2.5 days; discuss the implications for planning.
setup_requirements: MS Project, GanttPRO, or equivalent scheduling tool
Network diagram template (can be drawn by hand)
Historical project data for analogous estimating exercise

review.value: Any duration estimate for future work is probabilistic, not deterministic. Three-point estimation captures this uncertainty explicitly; presenting a single number implies a precision that does not exist. Use ranges in communication with stakeholders

Experiments show people consistently underestimate how long tasks take, even when they know they tend to underestimate. Historical actuals from similar past work are the strongest antidote; gut feel is the weakest

The dependencies you encode in the network define the shape of the schedule. A missed dependency (forgetting that legal review is required before launch) creates an impossible schedule. A false dependency (treating two parallel tasks as sequential) inflates duration unnecessarily

Breaking work into tiny activities gives precise tracking but adds overhead; leaving work in large chunks is easier to plan but hides progress. The 8-80 rule applies here too: activities of 8-80 hours balance granularity and overhead

class_schedule.topic: Critical Path Method and Schedule Compression
learning_objectives: Calculate early start, early finish, late start, late finish, and total float for all activities in a network
Identify the critical path and explain its significance for project duration
Apply schedule compression techniques: crashing and fast tracking, and evaluate their trade-offs
Distinguish CPM from PERT and apply PERT for probabilistic scheduling
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.4 (Planning) schedule subsections
Kerzner, Ch. 13 - Project Graphics (CPM and PERT)
Schwalbe, Ch. 6 - Schedule Development
Kelley and Walker, 'Critical-Path Planning and Scheduling' (1959) - original CPM paper

lecture_notes.introduction: The critical path method (CPM) is arguably the single most important analytical tool in project management. Developed in 1957 at DuPont and independently for the Polaris submarine program (as PERT), CPM identifies the longest chain of dependent activities in a network and reveals that this chain, and only this chain, determines the minimum project duration. Activities on the critical path have zero float (slack): any delay to any of them delays the entire project. Activities off the critical path have some amount of float and can slip without affecting the end date, up to a limit. The practical value is enormous: a PM who knows the critical path knows where to focus management attention, where to protect against delay, and where accelerating work will actually shorten the schedule. Conversely, adding resources to off-critical activities does nothing for project duration and wastes money. The forward pass calculates early start and early finish for every activity, working left to right through the network. The backward pass calculates late start and late finish, working right to left from the project end. Total float equals late start minus early start (or late finish minus early finish); any activity with zero total float is on the critical path. When the critical path's duration exceeds what the schedule allows, compression is needed. Two techniques exist. Crashing adds resources (overtime, more people) to critical activities at additional cost, trading money for time. Fast tracking overlaps activities that were originally sequential, accepting additional risk (overlapping design and construction means rework if design changes). Each has a cost and a limit: at some point, crashing yields diminishing returns (adding more cooks does not make the meal faster) and fast tracking creates unmanageable rework. The PM's job is to find the right mix. PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) extends CPM with probabilistic durations, giving expected project duration and variance, enabling statements like 'we have an 80% chance of finishing by March 15.'
key_concepts: Forward pass: Early Start (ES) and Early Finish (EF) calculated left to right; for each activity, ES = max of predecessor EFs, EF = ES + duration
Backward pass: Late Finish (LF) and Late Start (LS) calculated right to left; for each activity, LF = min of successor LSs, LS = LF - duration
Total Float: LS - ES = LF - EF; the amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project completion
Free Float: the amount an activity can be delayed without delaying the next activity
Critical path: the path with zero total float and longest duration; determines the project's minimum completion time
Near-critical path: a path with small float; can become critical if its activities are delayed slightly
Crashing: reducing duration of critical activities by adding resources at increased cost; select the critical activity with lowest crash cost per unit of time saved
Fast tracking: overlapping activities originally planned sequentially; no added cost but adds risk of rework
PERT: uses three-point estimates; expected duration = (O+4M+P)/6; variance = ((P-O)/6)^2; project variance = sum of variances along critical path
Probability of completion: using the normal approximation, P(complete by date T) can be calculated as z = (T - expected)/sqrt(project variance), then look up in standard normal table
Resource leveling: adjusting schedule to fit resource constraints; can extend project duration beyond CPM minimum
Schedule baseline: the approved schedule used for performance measurement; changes require formal control
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work a complete CPM problem. Network: A(3)->B(5), A(3)->C(2), B->D(4), C->D(4), B->E(7), D->F(3), E->F(3). Forward pass: A ES=0 EF=3; B ES=3 EF=8; C ES=3 EF=5; D ES=max(8,5)=8 EF=12; E ES=8 EF=15; F ES=max(12,15)=15 EF=18. Backward pass (working from F LF=18): F LS=15; E LF=15 LS=8; D LF=15 LS=11; C LF=11 LS=9; B LF=min(11,8)=8 LS=3; A LF=3 LS=0. Total float: A=0, B=0, C=6, D=3, E=0, F=0. Critical path: A-B-E-F, duration 18. Now compress: suppose we must finish in 16 days. Option 1 crash: reduce B from 5 to 3 (critical) at cost $2000 and reduce F from 3 to 2 at cost $1500; new duration 15 days, cost $3500. Option 2 fast track: overlap B and E by 2 days, accepting risk of rework; no cost but adds risk. Discuss the trade-off.
setup_requirements: Standard normal distribution table for PERT probabilities

review.key: Only the Critical Path Matters for Duration
review.value: Improvements to non-critical activities, no matter how dramatic, do not shorten the project. This is counterintuitive to managers who want to 'speed things up everywhere' and is a key insight that CPM teaches

Shortening the critical path can move another path into criticality. After any compression, recalculate the critical path. Multiple paths may be critical simultaneously, meaning multiple chains must all finish on time

When crashing, choose the critical activity with the lowest cost per day saved first, then continue until the target is met or crashing becomes infeasible. This produces the minimum-cost compression strategy

PERT's (O+4M+P)/6 formula comes from the beta distribution, which better represents typical task durations than a simple average. The factor of 4 on the most likely value reflects that most actual durations cluster near the most likely, not halfway between extremes

class_schedule.topic: Cost Management: Estimation, Budgeting, and Earned Value
learning_objectives: Apply cost estimation techniques at appropriate levels of project maturity (ROM, definitive)
Develop a project budget and cost baseline from WBS-level estimates and management reserves
Calculate earned value management (EVM) metrics: PV, EV, AC, SV, CV, SPI, CPI, EAC, ETC, VAC
Interpret EVM results to forecast project outcomes and identify corrective actions
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.5 (Delivery) and Section 2.7 (Measurement)
Kerzner, Ch. 14 - Pricing and Estimating; Ch. 15 - Cost Control
Schwalbe, Ch. 7 - Project Cost Management
PMI, Practice Standard for Earned Value Management, 2nd ed.

lecture_notes.introduction: Cost management is where project management meets finance. Executives do not approve projects based on Gantt charts; they approve them based on expected return on investment, and they want to know during execution whether the project is delivering that return. Cost estimation, budgeting, and earned value management provide the tools to answer these questions credibly. Cost estimates evolve in precision as the project matures. Early in the project, when scope is vague, a rough order of magnitude (ROM) estimate within -25% to +75% is appropriate. As the WBS develops and more detail emerges, estimates become definitive, tightening to -5% to +10%. Each estimate type has legitimate uses: ROM supports go/no-go decisions, definitive supports baseline approval and earned value tracking. Mixing them, presenting a ROM as if it were definitive, causes enormous problems downstream. The cost baseline is the time-phased budget, showing how much of the total budget is planned to be spent by each point in time, typically displayed as an S-curve. Once approved, this becomes the reference for measuring cost performance. Earned value management (EVM) is the gold standard for integrated cost and schedule performance measurement. It answers three questions at any point in the project: how much work did we plan to have done, how much did we actually do, and how much did we actually spend? PV (planned value), EV (earned value), and AC (actual cost) are the three fundamental numbers. Their relationships produce powerful indicators: cost variance (CV = EV - AC) shows whether we are over or under budget for work completed; schedule variance (SV = EV - PV) shows whether we are ahead or behind schedule; CPI (EV/AC) is the cost performance index; SPI (EV/PV) is the schedule performance index. EVM also provides forecasts: EAC (estimate at completion) predicts the final cost, ETC (estimate to complete) predicts remaining cost, VAC (variance at completion) predicts the final variance. Mastering EVM is a key professional skill and appears on virtually every PMP exam.
key_concepts: Rough Order of Magnitude (ROM): -25% to +75% accuracy; used for early screening and go/no-go decisions
Budget estimate: -10% to +25% accuracy; used for business case approval
Definitive estimate: -5% to +10% accuracy; used for baseline and control
Direct costs: costs directly attributable to the project (labor, materials, equipment)
Indirect costs: overhead, facilities, shared resources allocated to the project
Cost baseline: approved time-phased budget, typically an S-curve showing cumulative PV over time
Management reserve: budget held by management for unknown-unknown risks; not part of cost baseline
Contingency reserve: budget for known risks with assigned probability and impact; part of cost baseline
Planned Value (PV): budgeted cost of work scheduled at a point in time (BCWS in older terminology)
Earned Value (EV): budgeted cost of work actually performed (BCWP); takes credit based on progress not effort
Actual Cost (AC): actual cost incurred for work performed (ACWP)
Cost Variance (CV) = EV - AC; positive is under budget, negative is over budget
Schedule Variance (SV) = EV - PV; positive is ahead, negative is behind; expressed in dollars not time
Cost Performance Index (CPI) = EV/AC; 1.0 is on budget, less than 1 is over budget; 0.85 means getting 85 cents of work per dollar spent
Schedule Performance Index (SPI) = EV/PV; 1.0 is on schedule, less than 1 is behind
Estimate at Completion (EAC): multiple formulas; most common EAC = BAC/CPI assumes current cost performance continues
Estimate to Complete (ETC) = EAC - AC
Variance at Completion (VAC) = BAC - EAC; forecast of how much over or under budget the project will finish
To Complete Performance Index (TCPI) = (BAC - EV)/(BAC - AC); the CPI that must be achieved for remaining work to finish on budget
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete an EVM calculation from scratch. A 12-month, $1,200,000 project has planned linear spending of $100,000 per month. At end of month 4: PV = $400,000 (4 months times $100K). The team reports 30% of total work complete: EV = 0.30 times $1,200,000 = $360,000. Actual spending: AC = $420,000. Calculate: CV = EV - AC = 360,000 - 420,000 = -$60,000 (over budget). SV = EV - PV = 360,000 - 400,000 = -$40,000 (behind schedule). CPI = 360/420 = 0.857 (getting only 85.7 cents of value per dollar spent). SPI = 360/400 = 0.90 (only 90% of planned progress achieved). EAC assuming continued performance = BAC/CPI = 1,200,000/0.857 = $1,400,234 (projected $200K over budget). ETC = 1,400,234 - 420,000 = $980,234. VAC = 1,200,000 - 1,400,234 = -$200,234. Interpretation: project is in trouble; if nothing changes, it will finish $200K over budget. Corrective actions: scope reduction, productivity improvement, additional oversight, or replanning the baseline. Discuss why EVM is better than simple budget-vs-actual: at this point, a naive comparison would say 'we have spent $420K of $1.2M, so we are within budget' but we have only completed 30% of work when schedule said we should be 33% done.
setup_requirements: Case data: project cost baseline, actual reports at several time points
Excel or spreadsheet tool for calculations

review.value: Presenting a 50% confident estimate as a single number creates false precision that haunts the project. Early estimates should be ranges with explicit assumptions; precision comes as information develops

Every work package needs a rule for how to earn value: 0/100 (no credit until complete), 50/50 (50% at start, 50% at complete), percent complete, or milestone-based. Without these rules, EV is subjective and the numbers are meaningless

Research from the Defense Acquisition University shows that CPI tends to stabilize by 20% completion and rarely improves significantly after that. This is why EAC = BAC/CPI is a reasonable default: if you are 20% done and running at 0.85, you will likely finish at 0.85

Unlike simple budget tracking, EVM detects problems early enough to act. A CPI of 0.85 at 20% completion is a strong signal; waiting until 50% completion to notice the overrun leaves much less room to respond

class_schedule.topic: Risk Management: Identification, Analysis, and Response
learning_objectives: Distinguish individual project risks from overall project risk
Apply techniques for risk identification (brainstorming, SWOT, checklists, assumptions analysis)
Perform qualitative risk analysis using probability-impact matrix and quantitative analysis using Monte Carlo simulation
Develop risk responses for threats (avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept) and opportunities (exploit, share, enhance, accept)
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.8 (Uncertainty Performance Domain)
Kerzner, Ch. 17 - Risk Management
Schwalbe, Ch. 11 - Project Risk Management
Hillson, Managing Risk in Projects, 2nd ed., Ch. 1-4
PMI, Practice Standard for Project Risk Management

lecture_notes.introduction: Every project faces uncertainty. A risk is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a positive or negative effect on project objectives. Note the symmetry: risks include opportunities as well as threats. Good risk management increases the probability and impact of positive events and decreases the probability and impact of negative events. The alternative to risk management is not having fewer risks; it is being surprised by them. The Denver International Airport baggage system is a classic failure. The project assumed that an untested automated baggage system would work at unprecedented scale, a huge unidentified risk. When it did not, the airport opening was delayed 16 months and cost over $560 million in overruns. Proper risk management would have identified this risk, analyzed its probability and impact, and developed a response plan (including fallback to conventional baggage handling) before committing to the design. Risk management has six major processes: planning, identification, qualitative analysis, quantitative analysis, response planning, and monitoring. Identification uses structured techniques to surface risks the team has not explicitly considered: brainstorming, expert interviews, SWOT analysis, assumptions and constraints analysis, checklists from lessons learned, and root cause analysis. The output is a risk register, the living document of all identified risks. Qualitative analysis prioritizes risks by probability and impact, typically using a 5x5 probability-impact matrix; risks in the high-probability, high-impact corner get the most attention. Quantitative analysis, used for high-stakes projects, applies numerical techniques like Monte Carlo simulation to model the aggregated impact of many risks on project outcomes. Response planning develops specific strategies for each significant risk. Threats can be avoided (change the plan to eliminate the risk), transferred (insurance or contract), mitigated (reduce probability or impact), or accepted (document and watch). Opportunities can be exploited (make sure it happens), shared (partner to capture), enhanced (increase probability or impact), or accepted. Throughout execution, the risk register is reviewed regularly: new risks are identified, existing risks are reassessed, and responses are executed.
key_concepts: Risk: an uncertain event or condition with positive or negative effect on project objectives; has probability and impact
Known-knowns: things we know we know; not risks, they are facts to plan around
Known-unknowns: risks we have identified, with uncertain occurrence; managed via contingency reserves and response plans
Unknown-unknowns: things we do not know we do not know; managed via management reserves and rigorous identification processes
Risk identification techniques: brainstorming, Delphi technique, interviews, SWOT analysis, checklist analysis, assumption analysis, root cause analysis, diagramming (cause-effect, flowcharts)
Risk register: living document of each risk with ID, description, category, probability, impact, owner, response strategy, and status
Qualitative analysis: subjective rating of probability (e.g., 1-5) and impact (e.g., 1-5); probability-impact matrix assigns severity
Quantitative analysis: numerical techniques (Monte Carlo simulation, decision tree analysis, expected monetary value); used for large or complex projects
Monte Carlo simulation: runs thousands of schedule/cost trials with random sampling from probability distributions for uncertain activities; produces probability distribution of outcomes
Expected Monetary Value (EMV): probability times impact; useful for decision analysis
Threat responses: avoid (change plan to eliminate), transfer (contract, insurance), mitigate (reduce probability or impact), accept (watch and plan contingency)
Opportunity responses: exploit (ensure it happens), share (partner), enhance (increase probability or impact), accept
Escalation: risks outside PM authority are escalated to the sponsor or portfolio level
Secondary risks: new risks introduced by risk responses; must be identified and managed
Residual risks: what remains after responses are implemented; accepted explicitly
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a risk register for a 12-month software migration project. Identify 10 risks across categories: technical (legacy data format not fully understood, integration with third-party system may fail), schedule (vendor deliveries may be late), resource (lead architect may leave), external (regulatory change may require rework), organizational (sponsor retirement mid-project). For each, assess probability (1-5) and impact (1-5). The legacy data format risk is rated probability 4, impact 5 (total 20, critical). Build response: mitigate by doing a data discovery spike in weeks 1-3 to reduce probability to 2. Calculate EMV for a decision tree: build in-house (cost $500K, 70% success) vs buy vendor solution (cost $800K, 95% success). In-house EMV = 0.70 times value - $500K; vendor EMV = 0.95 times value - $800K. Walk through how Monte Carlo simulation would combine 20 activity durations with uncertainty distributions to produce a project completion probability curve.
setup_requirements: Excel with Monte Carlo add-in (@RISK, Crystal Ball, or simple VBA) or tool demo
Case study: Denver International Airport baggage system risks

review.value: A risk you have not identified cannot be managed. The best risk management processes invest heavily in identification: diverse teams, multiple techniques, historical checklists. The response planning is useless if the risks are not on the list

Risk management does not prevent bad events; it prevents surprises. When an identified risk materializes, the response plan is already documented and the team can execute. Unidentified risks cause the scrambling, overruns, and blame that doom projects

Most risk management focuses on threats, but the same techniques apply to opportunities. A project that captures opportunities systematically outperforms one that only defends against threats; this is where PM adds strategic value

Every risk in the register must have a single named owner responsible for monitoring and executing the response. Risks owned by 'the team' are owned by no one; accountability requires individual ownership

class_schedule.topic: Quality Management: Planning, Assurance, and Control
learning_objectives: Define quality planning, quality assurance, and quality control and distinguish their purposes
Apply quality tools: cause-effect diagrams, Pareto charts, control charts, and process capability analysis
Calculate the cost of quality (prevention, appraisal, internal failure, external failure)
Apply continuous improvement frameworks: PDCA, Six Sigma, and Lean principles
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.7 (Measurement); quality subsections in Section 2.4
Kerzner, Ch. 20 - Quality Management
Schwalbe, Ch. 8 - Project Quality Management
Juran, Quality Handbook (selected chapters)
Deming, Out of the Crisis, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality is conformance to requirements and fitness for use. Both matter: a project that delivers on time and on budget but fails to meet requirements has not delivered value. Quality management plans how quality will be achieved, assures that quality processes are followed, and controls that outputs meet specifications. These three are distinct: planning is strategic (what quality means for this project, what standards apply), assurance is process-focused (are we doing the right activities correctly), and control is product-focused (do the deliverables meet the standards). The cost of quality principle, articulated by Joseph Juran and Philip Crosby, reveals a counterintuitive truth: preventing defects is almost always cheaper than fixing them. The four categories of quality cost are prevention (training, design reviews, process improvement), appraisal (inspection, testing), internal failure (rework, scrap, defects found before delivery), and external failure (warranty claims, customer complaints, reputation damage). Studies consistently show the 1-10-100 rule: a defect that costs $1 to prevent costs $10 to fix in development and $100 to fix after release. The Japanese kaizen (continuous improvement) philosophy, embodied in the Toyota Production System, teaches that small incremental improvements compound over time into massive competitive advantage. The basic improvement loop is Deming's PDCA (Plan, Do, Check, Act): plan a change, do it on a small scale, check results, act to institutionalize or discard. Six Sigma, developed at Motorola and popularized by GE, applies rigorous statistical methods to reduce variation, targeting 3.4 defects per million opportunities. Lean, derived from Toyota, focuses on eliminating waste in processes. Both are now widely used in project environments beyond manufacturing. The quality tools, fishbone diagrams for root cause analysis, Pareto charts to focus on vital few causes, control charts to distinguish normal variation from special causes, and process capability indices to evaluate process performance, are practical techniques every PM should be able to apply.
key_concepts: Quality planning: identifying relevant standards (ISO 9001, customer specs, regulatory), defining quality objectives, and planning how to achieve them
Quality assurance (QA): auditing processes to ensure they meet standards; proactive, process-focused
Quality control (QC): inspecting outputs to ensure they meet specifications; reactive, product-focused
Quality policy: organization's commitment to quality, set by senior management
Cost of quality: prevention + appraisal + internal failure + external failure; prevention and appraisal are costs of conformance, failures are costs of nonconformance
1-10-100 rule: cost to fix defects escalates by factor of 10 at each stage (design to build to customer)
Continuous improvement (kaizen): incremental improvements continuously, rather than occasional large changes
PDCA cycle: Plan a change, Do it small-scale, Check results, Act to institutionalize; the foundation of iterative improvement
Six Sigma: DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology; targets 3.4 defects per million; belts from yellow to black
Lean: eliminates seven wastes (TIMWOOD: Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Overprocessing, Defects) plus underutilization of talent
Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram: visualizes causes of a problem grouped into categories (people, process, materials, equipment, environment, management)
Pareto chart: bar chart sorted by frequency with cumulative line; shows the 80/20 principle - 80% of problems come from 20% of causes
Control chart: time series with upper and lower control limits; points within limits are normal variation, points outside signal special cause
Process capability indices Cp and Cpk: measure how well a process fits within specification limits; Cpk greater than 1.33 is industry standard for capable process
Benchmarking: comparing project performance against best-in-class to identify improvement opportunities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a fishbone diagram for a problem: 'customer complaints about order errors.' Categories: People (training, turnover), Process (order entry workflow, verification), Technology (system bugs, UI confusion), Materials (catalog data quality), Measurement (error tracking, feedback loops), Environment (call center noise, shift scheduling). Then build a Pareto chart of error types from a real dataset: wrong quantity (45%), wrong item (30%), wrong address (15%), other (10%); vital few = quantity and item. Next, interpret a control chart showing daily defect rate: most days within control limits indicates stable process with common cause variation; a spike above the upper limit signals a special cause needing investigation. Compute cost of quality for a sample project: prevention $50K, appraisal $30K, internal failure $40K, external failure $200K, total $320K; if doubling prevention to $100K could reduce external failure to $50K, net savings = $110K annually, strong case for more investment in prevention.
setup_requirements: Excel for Pareto charts and control charts
Case study: Toyota production system or GE Six Sigma deployment

review.value: The 1-10-100 rule holds across industries. Investing in training, design reviews, and test automation costs pennies compared to field failures. Mature organizations shift investment to prevention over time and reap dramatically lower total quality costs

Deming famously said 'in God we trust; all others must bring data.' Before proposing a solution, measure the current state. Solutions to unmeasured problems are guesses; solutions to measured problems can be evaluated

Quality cannot be inspected into a product; it must be designed and built in. The PM's job is not to find defects after the fact but to put processes in place that prevent defects in the first place. Quality assurance beats quality control

A deliverable that meets the specification but does not serve the customer has not achieved quality. Quality planning starts with understanding what the customer values, not what is easy to produce. This is particularly important in agile projects where customer feedback shapes requirements iteratively

learning_objectives: Develop a resource management plan covering human and physical resources
Apply Tuckman's model (forming, storming, norming, performing, adjourning) to team development
Use motivational theories (Maslow, Herzberg, McGregor) to diagnose team dynamics and plan interventions
Resolve conflicts using Thomas-Kilmann modes and lead virtual and culturally diverse teams
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.2 (Team Performance Domain)
Kerzner, Ch. 6 - Management of Your Time and Stress; Ch. 7 - Conflict Management
Schwalbe, Ch. 9 - Project Resource Management
Lencioni, The Five Dysfunctions of a Team
HBR, 'Building a Virtual Team' (various articles)

lecture_notes.introduction: Projects get done by people, and people management is the hardest and most important skill for a project manager. Technical skills can be hired; leadership skills must be developed. The resource management process has two halves: planning the resources the project will need (roles, responsibilities, staffing, equipment, facilities), and developing and managing the people who do the work. The human side is where projects truly succeed or fail. Bruce Tuckman's model of team development (1965) identifies five stages every team passes through: forming (polite, uncertain), storming (conflict as roles and power emerge), norming (agreements established, collaboration begins), performing (high productivity, team works as a unit), and adjourning (project ends, team dissolves). A skilled PM recognizes which stage the team is in and intervenes appropriately. Storming looks like conflict but is actually a necessary stage; suppressing it stalls the team in polite unproductivity. Once the team is performing, major changes (new members, changed scope) can throw them back to forming. Motivation theory provides frameworks for understanding why team members engage or disengage. Maslow's hierarchy puts basic needs (physiological, safety) before higher needs (belonging, esteem, self-actualization); team members worried about layoffs cannot focus on creative work. Herzberg distinguishes hygiene factors (salary, working conditions, supervision) whose absence causes dissatisfaction but whose presence does not motivate, from motivators (achievement, recognition, responsibility, growth) that actually drive engagement. McGregor's Theory X and Theory Y contrasts managers who assume people are lazy and need control with managers who assume people want to do good work and need support; the manager's beliefs become self-fulfilling. Conflict is inevitable in projects and often productive if handled well. The Thomas-Kilmann framework identifies five modes: competing (my way), accommodating (your way), avoiding (no way), compromising (half way), collaborating (our way). Each is appropriate in different situations; the mature PM adapts. Virtual and cross-cultural teams add complexity: time zones, communication styles, cultural norms around hierarchy and disagreement, language nuances. Building trust across these barriers requires deliberate effort, frequent communication, and clear expectations.
key_concepts: Resource management plan: roles and responsibilities (RACI chart), organizational structure, staffing plan, training needs, rewards and recognition, safety
RACI matrix: Responsible (does the work), Accountable (one person, approves), Consulted (input before decision), Informed (notified of decisions)

Tuckman's model: Forming (team comes together, polite, uncertain), Storming (conflict emerges as real work begins), Norming (team establishes norms, cooperation increases), Performing (high productivity, self-managing), Adjourning (team completes project and disbands)
Maslow's hierarchy: physiological, safety, love/belonging, esteem, self-actualization; lower levels must be met before higher matter
Herzberg's two-factor theory: hygiene factors prevent dissatisfaction; motivators create satisfaction; they are independent, not opposites
McGregor Theory X/Y: X assumes people dislike work and need coercion; Y assumes people are self-motivated given the right conditions
Expectancy theory (Vroom): motivation = expectancy (will effort lead to performance) * instrumentality (will performance lead to outcome) * valence (is the outcome valued)

Thomas-Kilmann conflict modes: competing (assertive, uncooperative), collaborating (assertive, cooperative), compromising (middle), avoiding (unassertive, uncooperative), accommodating (unassertive, cooperative)
Servant leadership: leader serves the team by removing obstacles, providing resources, and enabling success; contrasted with command-and-control
Situational leadership (Hersey-Blanchard): directing, coaching, supporting, delegating; matched to follower readiness
Emotional intelligence (Goleman): self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, social skill; the foundation of effective leadership
Virtual team challenges: time zones, communication bandwidth, trust building, cultural differences, technology reliability
Hofstede's cultural dimensions: power distance, individualism/collectivism, masculinity/femininity, uncertainty avoidance, long-term orientation, indulgence
Psychological safety (Edmondson): team members feel safe to take interpersonal risks; Google's Project Aristotle identified it as the #1 predictor of high-performing teams
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a RACI matrix for a website launch: tasks (content creation, design, development, testing, deployment, marketing), team members (PM, marketing manager, designer, developer, QA lead, sponsor). Identify one R, one A, and appropriate C and I for each task. Discuss common mistakes (multiple As for one task, no R, everyone C). Then role-play a conflict scenario: a senior developer and a junior designer disagree about the user flow; walk through each Thomas-Kilmann mode and when each would be appropriate. Finally, examine a real virtual team case: a 15-person team spread across India, the UK, and the US working on a banking application; what communication norms, meeting schedules, and documentation practices enable success.
setup_requirements: Case study: Google Project Aristotle on team effectiveness

review.value: A team that skips the storming phase has not truly formed. Suppressing conflict in the name of harmony prevents the negotiations about roles, norms, and expectations that enable performing. Let productive conflict happen and facilitate its resolution

Raising salary above a dissatisfaction threshold is a hygiene improvement; it removes a complaint but does not energize people. Genuine motivation comes from meaningful work, recognition, and growth opportunities. Managers who think money alone motivates are repeatedly disappointed

Google's five-year study of high-performing teams found that psychological safety, the confidence that speaking up will not be punished, matters more than any other factor. A PM's most important leadership act may be modeling vulnerability and welcoming disagreement

In co-located teams, informal hallway conversation fills gaps. In virtual teams, nothing fills that gap unless you create structured alternatives: daily stand-ups, weekly one-on-ones, documented decisions. Over-communicate by the standards of a co-located team and you will be at parity virtually

learning_objectives: Plan project communications tailored to stakeholder needs and information requirements
Apply the communication model (sender, message, medium, noise, receiver, feedback) to diagnose communication breakdowns
Write effective project status reports and run productive meetings
Manage executive communication, escalation, and difficult conversations with stakeholders
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.2 and communications in 2.4
Kerzner, Ch. 8 - Project Management and Leadership; Ch. 9 - Communications Management
Schwalbe, Ch. 10 - Project Communications Management
Minto, The Pyramid Principle (executive communication)
HBR, 'The Secrets of Great Teamwork' (2016)

lecture_notes.introduction: Project managers spend 80-90% of their time communicating. This is not inefficiency; it is the work itself. A PM communicates to align stakeholders, direct the team, report status, negotiate changes, escalate problems, and capture learnings. When communication is effective, everything else becomes possible; when communication fails, even technically sound projects collapse. This session focuses on the practical craft of project communication: how to plan it, how to execute it, and how to handle the hard parts. The communications management plan is the written strategy for who gets what information, when, how, and from whom. For a small project, this may be a one-page table; for a major program with hundreds of stakeholders, it can be a substantial document. The plan answers key questions: what information does each stakeholder need, how frequently, in what format, through what channel, and who produces it. Poorly planned communication produces information overload for executives (inboxes full of details they cannot use) and information starvation for team members (who do not know what is changing around them). The communication model reveals where breakdowns occur: the sender encodes a message, which travels through a medium subject to noise, is decoded by the receiver, who provides feedback. Each step can fail: the sender can encode poorly (unclear writing, wrong terminology), the medium can introduce noise (a long email chain, cultural differences), the receiver can decode differently than intended. Active listening, feedback loops, and choosing the right medium for the message all counter these failure modes. Status reports are the PM's most repeated communication artifact. A good status report has a consistent structure: summary (single paragraph, the executive takeaway), schedule and cost status (with EVM or equivalent metrics), risks and issues (prioritized), decisions needed, upcoming activities. The temptation to sugarcoat status is strong and destructive: a late reporter of bad news becomes an unreliable reporter, and stakeholders will find out anyway. Executive communication requires the pyramid principle: lead with the answer, support with three reasons, conclude with details as needed. Burying the lead in executive conversations wastes everyone's time. Escalation is a specific skill: when something is outside the PM's authority or stalled, the PM must escalate clearly without blaming, provide options, and request a specific decision.
key_concepts: Communications management plan: stakeholder information needs, format, frequency, method, sender, purpose; lives as part of the project management plan
Information distribution methods: push (email, reports), pull (shared workspace, dashboards), interactive (meetings, calls)
Number of communication channels: n(n-1)/2 for n people; a 10-person team has 45 channels; exponential growth explains why large teams communicate badly
Communication model: sender encodes, message travels via medium with noise, receiver decodes, feedback loop closes the cycle
Active listening: focusing fully on the speaker, paraphrasing to confirm understanding, suspending judgment until the speaker finishes
Nonverbal communication: tone, facial expression, body language; 55-65% of meaning in face-to-face conversation is nonverbal (Mehrabian)
Status report structure: RAG (red/amber/green) summary, key metrics (schedule, cost, scope), risks and issues, decisions needed, next period plan
Executive communication: pyramid principle (answer first, support below, details last); executives have minutes not hours for your report
Meeting management: agenda distributed in advance, clear purpose and desired outcomes, time-boxed, minutes with action items and owners
Standup meetings: daily 15-minute sync used in agile teams; each member answers what I did, what I will do, what is blocking me
Escalation: when to escalate (when it exceeds PM authority or stalls progress), how to escalate (clear issue, options, recommendation, specific ask)
Difficult conversations: Stone, Patton, and Heen's framework: what happened, feelings, identity; most difficult conversations involve all three layers
Information radiators: physical or digital displays of current project status (burndown charts, Kanban boards) accessible to all stakeholders
Lessons learned communication: capturing and sharing learnings across projects, not just at end but continuously
lecture_notes.demonstration: Draft a one-page status report for a hypothetical project: 60% complete, CPI 0.92 (slightly over budget), SPI 0.95 (slightly behind), 3 open high-priority risks, 2 decisions needed from sponsor this week. Apply the pyramid principle: top line 'Project is AMBER: trending to deliver 2 weeks late and 5% over budget without intervention.' Then three supporting sections: cost (EVM metrics), schedule (milestones achieved and missed), risks (top three with current status). Recommendation: approve scope reduction for module X to recover schedule and cost; needed by end of this week. Then walk through a difficult conversation: a team member is underperforming and the PM must address it. Use the three-layer framework: what happened (specific examples), feelings (impact on team and project), identity (this is a learning moment, not a judgment of worth). Demonstrate the difference between attacking and confronting effectively.

review.value: Good news by email, bad news by phone or in person. A terse email can sound angry when it was meant to be efficient. For complex or emotional topics, pick the medium that preserves the most information and human connection

Stakeholders can handle bad news if they know about it early. What they cannot handle is being surprised. The PM's job is to alert stakeholders to emerging problems before they become crises, even at the risk of being a bearer of uncomfortable news

At phase boundaries, scope changes, or team changes, communication needs temporarily multiply. The information everyone needs to realign is not yet widely distributed. Plan for this spike with extra meetings, additional written summaries, and explicit decision records

Decisions made in a meeting drift by the next meeting unless documented. A simple 'Decision Log' that captures who decided what, when, and why, referenced in subsequent meetings, prevents weeks of rework from unrecorded decisions

learning_objectives: Conduct make-or-buy analysis and plan project procurements
Compare contract types (fixed price, cost reimbursable, time and materials) and their risk implications for buyer and seller
Execute the procurement process: bid documents, source selection, contract award, and administration
Manage vendor relationships, performance, and contract closure
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, procurement in Section 2.5
Kerzner, Ch. 18 - Contract Management; Ch. 19 - Procurement Management
Schwalbe, Ch. 12 - Project Procurement Management
Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) selected sections for US government contracts

lecture_notes.introduction: Few projects are built entirely with internal resources. Most depend on suppliers, subcontractors, consultants, software vendors, and other external parties. Procurement management is the discipline of obtaining products and services from outside the project team, governed by contracts that allocate risk, responsibility, and reward between buyer and seller. The contract is a legal document, but it is also a management tool: it defines what the vendor will deliver, how performance will be measured, and what happens when things go wrong. A well-crafted contract prevents many disputes by clarifying expectations; a poorly crafted contract creates them. The first decision is make-or-buy: should this work be done internally or contracted out? Factors include cost comparison, core competency (is this what we are uniquely good at), capacity (do we have time and people), quality expectations, and risk transfer. Once the decision to buy is made, the procurement process begins: develop requirements, write bid documents, solicit proposals, evaluate bids, negotiate, and award a contract. Contract type is the most consequential decision. Fixed price (firm-fixed-price, FFP) shifts cost risk to the seller: the buyer pays a set amount regardless of seller's actual cost. This works when scope is well-defined and the seller can accurately estimate. Cost-reimbursable contracts (cost plus fixed fee, cost plus incentive fee, cost plus award fee) shift cost risk to the buyer: the seller is reimbursed for actual costs plus a fee. This works for research or exploratory work where scope cannot be precisely defined. Time and materials (T&M) is a hybrid: fixed hourly rates with variable hours, typically for professional services engagements with uncertain duration. Each contract type creates different seller incentives, and the PM must understand these to manage effectively. A fixed-price contract encourages the seller to cut corners if the scope expands; a cost-reimbursable contract encourages the seller to maximize billable hours. Source selection uses weighted criteria: technical capability, past performance, cost, management approach, and others. The best value is not always the lowest cost; a cheap vendor who fails costs more than an expensive one who succeeds. After award, contract administration manages performance: monitoring deliverables against specifications, processing payments, handling change orders, and resolving disputes. At project end, formal contract closure ensures all obligations are met and all payments settled.
key_concepts: Make-or-buy analysis: compare internal cost, capability, capacity, and strategic fit against external options
Procurement management plan: what will be procured, contract types, vendor management approach, risk allocation, metrics
Statement of Work (SOW): detailed description of what the seller will deliver, including specifications, deliverables, schedule, performance criteria
Bid documents: Request for Information (RFI), Request for Proposal (RFP), Request for Quote (RFQ), Invitation for Bid (IFB)
Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP): seller commits to deliver specified scope for a fixed price; lowest buyer risk, highest seller risk
Fixed-Price Incentive Fee (FPIF): fixed price with incentive for cost performance; shared risk
Fixed-Price with Economic Price Adjustment (FP-EPA): fixed price with inflation adjustment; used for long contracts
Cost Plus Fixed Fee (CPFF): seller's actual costs plus a fixed dollar fee; buyer bears cost risk
Cost Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF): actual costs plus incentive fee based on target cost achievement
Cost Plus Award Fee (CPAF): actual costs plus subjective award fee based on performance criteria
Time and Materials (T&M): hourly rates for labor, cost plus markup for materials; used for uncertain-duration work
Privity of contract: legal relationship between contracting parties; subcontractors have no direct relationship with the original buyer
Force majeure: clause releasing parties from obligations due to extraordinary events (natural disasters, war, pandemics)
Change orders: formal modifications to contracts, typically requiring written agreement and price adjustment
Warranties: seller's guarantees about performance or fitness for purpose; limited by express and implied warranty law
Liquidated damages: pre-agreed amount payable on breach of contract (e.g., late delivery); must be reasonable forecast of actual damages to be enforceable
Contract closure: formal verification that all terms are met, all deliverables accepted, all payments made, all claims resolved
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare contract types for a software development engagement. Scenario: develop a mobile banking app, 6 months, scope defined in detail. Option A: fixed price at $800K, vendor accepts scope risk, if actual cost exceeds $800K, vendor absorbs the loss. Option B: cost plus 10% fee, vendor's actual cost becomes our cost, say $750K, we pay $825K; if actual is $1M we pay $1.1M. Discuss risk allocation: FFP is better if scope is stable but forces vendor to pad estimates (typical FFP bid = expected cost * 1.3 for buffer). Cost plus is better when scope is uncertain but removes vendor's cost discipline. Apply to a second scenario: research project developing an AI model with unpredictable scope. Here cost plus is more honest; an FFP vendor would either refuse to bid or bid so high it covers the worst case. Role-play a source selection committee evaluating three vendor proposals on weighted criteria: technical 40%, management 20%, cost 30%, past performance 10%. Compute weighted scores and select the best value vendor.

review.value: The choice of contract type is fundamentally a choice of who bears what risk. No contract eliminates risk; it transfers risk between parties. A PM who does not understand the incentives misaligned with the risk allocation will be constantly fighting the contract structure

Lowest cost bids are often cheapest for a reason: cut corners, inexperienced staff, optimistic assumptions. Evaluating best value rather than lowest cost avoids the trap of vendor failures that cost far more than the savings

Most vendor disputes trace back to a vague SOW. Time spent making the SOW precise, unambiguous, and complete is the single best investment in procurement success. An excellent contract with a vague SOW is worth less than a mediocre contract with a precise one

A signed contract is not the end of procurement; it is the beginning. Active vendor management, regular performance reviews, proactive issue resolution, and building genuine working relationships produce far better outcomes than waiting for problems and invoking contract penalties

learning_objectives: Compare predictive (waterfall) and adaptive (agile) lifecycles and identify when each is appropriate
Explain the Agile Manifesto values and principles and the Scrum framework in detail
Describe Kanban, XP, SAFe, and LeSS and their use cases
Design a hybrid approach combining predictive and adaptive elements for a specific project context
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.3 (Development Approach and Lifecycle Performance Domain)
PMI and Agile Alliance, Agile Practice Guide
Schwaber and Sutherland, The Scrum Guide (latest version)
Kerzner, Ch. 21 - Agile Project Management
Beck et al., 'Manifesto for Agile Software Development' (2001)

lecture_notes.introduction: For decades, project management was synonymous with predictive planning: define scope upfront, plan in detail, execute the plan, deliver at the end. This works well when requirements are stable and the path is known: building a bridge, constructing a factory, rolling out standardized training. But when requirements are uncertain, when the market moves, when the technology is new, when the customer cannot fully articulate what they want until they see it, predictive planning creates a fiction: the plan is detailed but wrong, and rigid execution of a wrong plan guarantees failure. The Agile Manifesto, written by 17 software developers in 2001, articulated an alternative. Its four values prioritize individuals and interactions over processes and tools, working software over comprehensive documentation, customer collaboration over contract negotiation, and responding to change over following a plan. These are comparative statements, not absolutes: documentation still matters, but working software matters more when you must choose. Agile delivers work in short iterations (1-4 weeks), at the end of which working product increments are demonstrated to stakeholders, feedback is collected, and plans for the next iteration are adjusted. This continuous feedback loop handles uncertainty by refusing to commit to a long plan; instead, it commits to a process of continuous discovery. Scrum is the most widely used agile framework. It defines three roles (product owner, scrum master, developers), five events (sprint, sprint planning, daily scrum, sprint review, sprint retrospective), and three artifacts (product backlog, sprint backlog, increment). Kanban is a lighter-weight agile approach focused on visualizing work, limiting work in progress, and continuous flow rather than fixed iterations. Extreme Programming (XP) adds engineering practices (test-driven development, continuous integration, pair programming) to the agile mix. For larger organizations, scaling frameworks like SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework) and LeSS (Large-Scale Scrum) coordinate many agile teams toward common goals. The reality in most organizations is hybrid: some work is done predictively, some agilely, and the PM must design the approach that fits the context. A construction project with a compliance-heavy scope may use waterfall for the physical build and agile for the associated software. A product development effort may use agile for feature development within a predictive overall program schedule. The mature PM does not advocate dogmatically for one approach; they tailor based on context.
key_concepts: Predictive (waterfall) lifecycle: detailed upfront planning, sequential phases, scope fixed, time and cost flexible, delivery at end
Iterative lifecycle: repeated cycles of refinement on the same product; scope grows more precise with each iteration
Incremental lifecycle: deliverable built in pieces, each piece a working subset of the final product
Adaptive (agile) lifecycle: iterative and incremental, short iterations (1-4 weeks), customer feedback drives plan changes
Hybrid lifecycle: combines predictive and adaptive elements; e.g., predictive planning overall with adaptive delivery within phases
Agile Manifesto values: individuals and interactions, working software, customer collaboration, responding to change; each preferred over the alternative

Agile Manifesto principles (12): customer satisfaction through early delivery, welcome change, frequent delivery, business and developer collaboration, motivated individuals, face-to-face conversation, working software as primary metric, sustainable pace, technical excellence, simplicity, self-organizing teams, regular reflection and adjustment
Scrum roles: Product Owner (owns the backlog, maximizes value), Scrum Master (facilitates, removes impediments, serves the team), Developers (build the increment, self-organize)

Scrum events: Sprint (1-4 week container), Sprint Planning (what and how for this sprint), Daily Scrum (15-minute team sync), Sprint Review (demo to stakeholders, gather feedback), Sprint Retrospective (team reflection on process improvement)
Scrum artifacts: Product Backlog (ordered list of everything that might be done), Sprint Backlog (subset selected for this sprint plus plan), Increment (working product at end of sprint)
Definition of Done: team's shared agreement on what 'complete' means for a backlog item; ensures consistent quality
User stories: lightweight requirements in the form 'As a [role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]'; refined via acceptance criteria
Story points: relative sizing of stories, often using Fibonacci sequence; measures complexity not time
Velocity: average story points completed per sprint by a team; used for planning, not as a performance measure across teams
Kanban principles: visualize workflow, limit work in progress (WIP), manage flow, make policies explicit, implement feedback loops, improve collaboratively
Extreme Programming (XP): pair programming, test-driven development, continuous integration, collective code ownership, simple design, refactoring
SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework): portfolio, large solution, program, and team levels; Agile Release Trains coordinate multiple teams
LeSS (Large-Scale Scrum): scales Scrum with minimal additional process; retains most Scrum elements for multiple teams
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a Scrum cycle for a banking app team. Sprint length 2 weeks. Product backlog has 50 items; product owner has ordered them by value and dependency. Sprint planning: team picks top items totaling 40 story points (their velocity). They discuss each and agree on how to build it. During the sprint, daily scrums identify impediments (the external API is down, someone is blocked on legal review).
