Sprint review: team demos the 8 completed stories to the product owner and other stakeholders, who provide feedback. The tenth story is incomplete and returns to the backlog. Sprint retrospective: team discusses what went well (good collaboration on story 3) and what to improve (estimates were optimistic for story 7, we need more refinement before planning). Next sprint starts the following Monday. Then compare with a predictive approach: the same work planned upfront in a Gantt chart, detailed WBS, milestone deliverables. Discuss which works better for this project and why. Introduce the concept of a hybrid: the regulatory compliance portion uses predictive planning (must pass audit at fixed date), while feature development uses agile.
setup_requirements: Sample product backlog for class exercise

review.value: Critics sometimes claim agile is 'no plan, just coding.' In reality, agile demands more rigor in some ways than waterfall: continuous planning, constant feedback, disciplined retrospectives, rigorous definition of done. What it rejects is the illusion of a perfect upfront plan for uncertain work

The single most useful heuristic for choosing predictive or adaptive is the level of requirements uncertainty. Known requirements with stable environments favor predictive; uncertain requirements or changing environments favor adaptive. Most projects have both and benefit from hybrid

The three Scrum roles separate concerns that are often conflated: the product owner decides what (value), developers decide how (execution), the scrum master serves the process. Combining these (a PM who also acts as product owner and scrum master) recreates the pathologies Scrum was designed to solve

The retrospective is the meta-process that makes all other processes better. Teams that skip or phone in retrospectives stop improving and eventually stagnate. Teams that take retrospectives seriously improve continuously and compound over time

learning_objectives: Direct and manage project work against the baseline plan
Monitor and control scope, schedule, cost, quality, risks, and procurements
Apply integrated change control to evaluate and approve changes
Use dashboards, reports, and reviews to maintain stakeholder awareness and project alignment
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.5 (Delivery) and Section 2.7 (Measurement)
Kerzner, Ch. 15 - Cost Control; Ch. 16 - Trade-off Analysis
Schwalbe, Ch. 4.6 - Monitoring and Controlling
Project Management Journal, selected articles on earned schedule

lecture_notes.introduction: Execution is where the plan meets reality. No plan survives contact with execution unchanged; the PM's job during execution is to monitor what is actually happening, compare it against the plan, and take corrective action when the two diverge. This is not a failure of planning; it is the normal, expected rhythm of projects. The monitoring and controlling process runs continuously throughout execution, not just at milestone boundaries. Key monitoring activities include tracking scope (are we delivering what was agreed?), schedule (are we on time?), cost (are we on budget?), quality (are deliverables meeting standards?), risk (are new risks emerging?), stakeholder engagement (are people still aligned?), and resources (are we using people and equipment as planned?). Each of these has specific metrics and tools: EVM for cost and schedule, defect density for quality, risk register updates for risk, stakeholder engagement surveys for stakeholder health. Integrated change control is the formal process for evaluating proposed changes to the baseline. Changes come from all directions: customers ask for new features, team members discover missing requirements, the environment changes (regulations, markets, technology), risks materialize and require response. Every change must be evaluated for impact on scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk before approval. The change control board (CCB) reviews changes above a threshold and approves, defers, or rejects them. Smaller changes within the PM's authority can be approved by the PM. The integrity of the baseline depends on this discipline: uncontrolled changes produce plans that bear no relationship to reality, and EVM and progress metrics become meaningless. Corrective action is the work done to bring performance back into alignment with the plan; preventive action addresses anticipated deviations before they occur; defect repair fixes nonconformances. All are change requests and go through change control. Reports and dashboards are the PM's tools for staying aware and keeping others informed. A daily dashboard keeps the team synchronized; a weekly status report updates stakeholders; a monthly steering committee review engages executives. Each has different granularity and audience but should be consistent so that the executive view rolls up cleanly from the team view.
key_concepts: Execute the plan: follow the baseline while remaining responsive to learning and change
Monitoring: collecting data about project performance against the plan
Controlling: comparing actual to plan and taking action to restore alignment
Integrated change control: evaluating change impact across all performance domains before approval
Change control board (CCB): cross-functional group authorized to approve significant changes
Change request: formal proposal documenting the change, justification, and impact analysis
Corrective action: work performed to bring expected performance back in line with the plan
Preventive action: work performed to reduce likelihood of future deviations
Defect repair: modification to fix a nonconforming component
Scope verification: formal acceptance of deliverables by the customer or sponsor
Scope control: preventing unauthorized scope changes and managing authorized changes
Schedule variance trend: tracking SV over time to distinguish random variation from systemic drift
Earned schedule: extension of EVM that expresses schedule performance in time units rather than dollars
Issue log: tracks problems requiring action; issues are events that have happened, distinct from risks which have not yet
Dashboard design: at-a-glance view of key metrics; traffic light indicators, trend arrows, threshold alerts
Status review cadence: daily team standup, weekly PM status report, biweekly stakeholder update, monthly steering committee
Root cause analysis techniques: 5 whys, fishbone, Pareto, cause-effect matrix
Management by exception: focus management attention on deviations beyond agreed thresholds, not routine activities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a realistic change request scenario. Mid-project, the sponsor requests a new feature: 'integrate with our CRM system.' The PM runs impact analysis: scope increase equivalent to 3 work packages, schedule impact 4 weeks if critical path, cost impact $85,000, risk: integration complexity unknown. The PM presents options to the sponsor: (a) accept with 4-week delay and $85K addition, (b) substitute this feature for the existing reporting module deferring that, (c) defer the CRM integration to phase 2. The sponsor chooses option (b). Change control updates baseline: WBS, schedule, budget, risk register, communications plan. All stakeholders are notified of the scope change. The PM now monitors the new baseline, not the old one. Discuss how failing to update the baseline would corrupt all subsequent EVM calculations. Then demonstrate a daily standup: each team member reports what they completed yesterday, what they will do today, and what is blocking them. The PM captures impediments and works to remove them before the next standup.
setup_requirements: Dashboard mock-up showing schedule, cost, risks, and issues
Case study: a project that lost control of changes

review.value: Status reports are the output of monitoring, not the purpose. The purpose is early detection of deviation. A PM who only looks at status at reporting time is always behind; a PM who monitors continuously catches problems when they are still small

review.key: Change Control is Not Change Prevention
review.value: Change control does not mean rejecting changes; it means evaluating them explicitly before accepting them. A rigid 'no changes allowed' policy fails as badly as 'anything goes.' The goal is informed decisions about changes, not zero changes

Correcting a symptom without addressing the root cause produces temporary improvement and recurring problems. The 5 whys technique or fishbone diagram forces investigation beyond the first answer to the underlying driver

Management by exception focuses limited attention on what is off-track, trusting the rest to run as planned. Dashboards that highlight deviations (red/yellow/green) enable this; dashboards that treat every metric equally overload the viewer and bury the signal

learning_objectives: Apply integration management across all project management knowledge areas
Close phases and projects through formal acceptance, transition, and administrative closure
Conduct lessons learned sessions and integrate learnings into the organization's knowledge base
Transition deliverables to operations and release resources in a disciplined way
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 2.4 (Project Work) and integration discussions throughout
Kerzner, Ch. 22 - Closure
Schwalbe, Ch. 4 - Project Integration Management
Williams, 'Identifying Success Factors in Construction Projects' (International Journal of Project Management)

lecture_notes.introduction: Integration management is the work of tying everything together. It is easy to focus on individual knowledge areas, schedule, cost, scope, quality, as separate disciplines, but in reality they are deeply interconnected. A scope change affects schedule, cost, and risk. A schedule slip affects budget and stakeholder expectations. A quality problem affects scope and cost. Integration management ensures that these interactions are managed holistically, not as disconnected silos. The PM is the only role with accountability for the integrated view; functional managers and subject matter experts see their piece clearly but not the whole. Throughout the project, integration manifests in the project management plan (a single integrated plan encompassing all subsidiary plans), in integrated change control (changes evaluated across all dimensions), and in the PM's ongoing work of resolving conflicts and balancing competing demands. Project closing is often neglected because by the time execution ends, everyone is tired and eager to move on. But good closing is essential: it captures value from the experience, protects the organization from future claims, and sets up operations for success. Administrative closure includes formal acceptance of deliverables by the customer (sign-off), archiving of project documentation, releasing team members and resources, closing contracts and making final payments, and conducting a project debrief or postmortem. Lessons learned are the project's gift to the organization's future projects. A disciplined lessons learned process identifies what worked (so it can be repeated), what did not (so it can be avoided), and what surprised the team (so future teams will be prepared). Lessons learned are most valuable when they are specific, actionable, and accessible: 'improve communication' is useless; 'for cross-functional projects over 10 people, establish a weekly decision forum with documented decisions within 24 hours' is actionable. Transitioning deliverables to operations requires preparation: operations staff must be trained, documentation must be complete, transition runbooks must exist, ownership must be unambiguous. A sloppy transition generates problems that blame the project for years after it ended. Finally, the PM should celebrate the team and recognize contributions; this is both humane and practical, as it sustains people through the next project.
key_concepts: Integration management: coordinating all project management activities and knowledge areas as a unified whole
Project management plan: the integrated document combining all subsidiary plans (scope, schedule, cost, quality, resource, communication, risk, procurement, stakeholder)
Direct and manage project work: carrying out the project plan, producing deliverables, addressing issues
Manage project knowledge: using existing knowledge and creating new knowledge for project objectives
Monitor and control project work: tracking progress and taking corrective action
Close project or phase: finalizing all activities, formal acceptance, release of resources
Administrative closure: archiving documentation, finalizing budget and schedule records, closing contracts, updating organizational assets
Formal acceptance: signed document from customer confirming deliverables meet specifications
Lessons learned: documented experiences from the project that can improve future performance
Lessons learned register: living document updated throughout the project, not just at the end
Project postmortem: structured meeting to review what went right, what went wrong, and what to do differently
Postmortem anti-patterns: blame sessions, vague lessons ('communicate better'), unactionable findings, no follow-through
Transition plan: how deliverables move from project team to operational owners; includes training, documentation, handover criteria
Contract closure: formal verification that all contract obligations are met and payments settled
Resource release: team members and physical resources returned to functional managers or next assignments
Team recognition: formal acknowledgment of contributions; essential for morale and future recruiting
Knowledge transfer: ensuring operations and future projects can benefit from the team's accumulated knowledge
Project success factors: clear goals, stakeholder support, skilled team, risk management, communication, leadership
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a mock lessons learned session for a completed project. Use the structure: (1) what did we set out to do and did we achieve it, (2) what went well, (3) what did not go well, (4) what was surprising, (5) what would we do differently, (6) what recommendations do we have for future projects. The team identifies concrete examples: the early stakeholder workshop in week 2 was highly effective, should be standard; the vendor selection process took 3 weeks longer than planned because of legal review cycles, future projects should engage legal from week 1. Document specific, actionable recommendations. Then walk through a closeout checklist: final deliverable acceptance (signed), all change requests closed, all contracts closed, all invoices paid, final status report published, team recognition planned, project archive stored, lessons learned entered in knowledge base. Discuss the case of the Big Dig (Boston Central Artery/Tunnel) and how its closure processes captured lessons that shaped subsequent megaprojects.
setup_requirements: Sample postmortem document from a real project

review.value: Engineers integrate technical components; PMs integrate everything else. Schedule, cost, scope, quality, risk, people, stakeholders, each affects the others, and keeping the whole coherent is the unique value the PM brings. Losing sight of integration means losing sight of the project itself

Projects that end sloppily produce residual problems for months or years: unresolved contract disputes, confused operations, lost institutional knowledge, team members with no closure. A disciplined close is a gift to the organization's future

Organizations that capture lessons learned but never use them are no better than those that do not capture them. The value comes from accessible, actionable lessons that future projects consult during their planning. Create the process that makes this happen

Project teams pour themselves into difficult work. Recognizing that contribution, even a simple team dinner and thank-you, pays dividends in morale, retention, and willingness to join future projects. The most practical form of caring is also the most human

class_schedule.topic: Project Governance, Ethics, and Course Synthesis
learning_objectives: Explain project governance structures and their role in project oversight
Apply the PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct to common ethical dilemmas in project management
Synthesize the PMBOK performance domains into a coherent approach for tailoring PM practice to context
Identify paths for professional development: PMP certification, continuing education, and career progression
readings: PMBOK Guide 7, Section 1 (System for Value Delivery) and ethics content
PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct
Kerzner, Ch. 3 - Organizational Structures; Ch. 4 - Organizing and Staffing the Project Office
HBR, 'Why Good Projects Fail Anyway' (Matta and Ashkenas, 2003)

lecture_notes.introduction: We have covered fifteen sessions of project management practice, from initiation through closing, from scope and schedule through quality and risk. This final session situates the PM role within the broader organizational system: governance structures that oversee projects, the ethical obligations that bind professional project managers, and the paths for continued growth after this course. Governance is the set of structures and processes by which organizations direct and oversee project activities. At the organizational level, governance includes portfolio management (choosing which projects to do), program management (coordinating related projects), and project-level oversight (steering committees, sponsor roles, stage gates). A healthy governance structure provides clear authority, decision rights, and escalation paths; a broken governance structure produces projects that drift without direction or get pulled in conflicting directions by competing stakeholders. Stage gate reviews (sometimes called phase gates or tollgates) are formal checkpoints between phases where continuation is evaluated; each gate is a decision point to proceed, pivot, or stop. Organizations with disciplined stage gate processes kill projects that have lost their business case and reallocate resources to more promising work. Ethics in project management is often tested in situations where the right action is costly: reporting bad news that will embarrass the sponsor, refusing to sign off on a deliverable that does not meet specifications despite pressure, declining a vendor gift that could be construed as a bribe, disclosing a conflict of interest that might cost the assignment. The PMI Code of Ethics is built on four values: responsibility (taking ownership of decisions and their consequences), respect (treating people and property with due regard), fairness (making decisions impartially), and honesty (truthful communication and conduct). Each value has aspirational and mandatory standards; mandatory standards are enforceable by PMI. In a career, the PM will face ethical dilemmas; a strong foundation in these values and a willingness to act on them at personal cost is what separates professionals from mere technicians. Synthesizing the course: project management is a discipline, not a recipe. The PMBOK Guide provides principles and performance domains, not processes to follow mechanically. A skilled PM reads the context (project size, complexity, domain, organization culture, team maturity, customer type) and tailors the approach accordingly. The same PM might use rigorous waterfall for a regulated medical device development and light agile for a marketing website redesign, and both are correct. Professional development continues after this course. The Project Management Professional (PMP) certification from PMI is the global standard; the Certified Associate in Project Management (CAPM) is the entry-level version. Continuing education, industry events (PMI chapters, conferences), reading (HBR, PMI publications, industry journals), mentoring, and deliberate practice all contribute to PM growth. The most successful PMs treat their practice the way a doctor treats medicine: a commitment to lifelong learning as new research, techniques, and challenges emerge.
key_concepts: Project governance: framework, functions, and processes for project direction and oversight
Portfolio management: selecting and prioritizing the mix of projects aligned with strategy
Program management: coordinating related projects to deliver benefits unachievable by individual projects
Stage gates: formal checkpoints between phases where continuation is evaluated (go/no-go/modify)
Project sponsor role: champions the project, provides resources, makes strategic decisions, removes obstacles
Project Management Office (PMO): organizational structure that standardizes project governance and supports project execution (supportive, controlling, or directive)
Organizational structures: functional, matrix (weak, balanced, strong), projectized; each affects PM authority and project dynamics
PMI Code of Ethics values: responsibility, respect, fairness, honesty
Responsibility: own the consequences of decisions, protect proprietary information, fulfill commitments
Respect: show regard for others, self, resources; no abusive behavior
Fairness: transparent decision-making, disclose conflicts of interest, avoid favoritism
Honesty: truthful in communications and conduct, do not deceive, do not engage in dishonest behavior for personal gain
Common ethical dilemmas: scope sign-off pressure, gift acceptance from vendors, padding estimates, concealing bad news, conflict of interest
PMP certification: globally recognized, requires experience hours, education, exam, and continuing certification requirements (CCRs)
CAPM certification: entry-level, less experience required, based on PMBOK Guide
Professional development: continuous learning through courses, reading, communities of practice, mentoring, reflection
Tailoring: adjusting PM practice to fit project context; PMBOK 7 explicitly emphasizes this over one-size-fits-all process
Adaptive leadership: shifting style based on team maturity, task complexity, and situation dynamics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Present several ethical dilemmas and facilitate a structured discussion. Case 1: Your vendor offers you tickets to a major sporting event after winning a contract. Is acceptance appropriate? Discuss PMI code guidance, organizational policy, appearance of impropriety. Case 2: The sponsor tells you privately to omit the risk of regulatory non-compliance from the executive status report because 'it would rattle the board.' Walk through the responsibility to communicate accurately and the path for escalation if the sponsor persists. Case 3: A team member padded the schedule estimate by 50% and now the project is beating the 'plan'; do you tell the sponsor? Case 4: A former colleague now works for a vendor bidding on your project and shares internal information about your evaluation criteria. Discuss conflict of interest, source selection integrity, and the appropriate action. After the ethics discussion, synthesize the course: review the performance domains covered, connect them to real projects students have observed, and discuss how the same framework applies differently in different contexts. Finally, preview the PMP and CAPM paths, the PMI chapter in the local area, and recommended readings for continued growth.
setup_requirements: PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct (handout)
Career progression chart showing PM roles and certifications

review.value: Governance that is too heavy stifles execution; governance that is too light loses control. Effective governance provides structure at the right level: enough oversight to catch drift, enough freedom to let teams work. Finding that balance is an organizational skill that mature PMOs develop over years

review.key: Ethics at the Moment of Pressure
review.value: Ethics are easy when nothing is at stake. The test comes when honesty costs money, reputation, or the project itself. The PM who acts ethically under pressure builds a reputation that pays back for a career; the one who compromises builds a reputation that haunts them

There is no universal best practice; there is only best practice for a given context. A skilled PM reads the situation and adapts. Copying practices from one context to another without adaptation produces the cargo cult of project management, process without purpose

The PM profession evolves continuously: new methods (agile a generation ago, AI-assisted planning now), new tools, new regulatory contexts, new industries. A PM who stops learning after certification becomes obsolete. A PM who continues learning stays relevant for forty years

quizzes.topic: Project Initiation, Charter, Stakeholders, and Scope

questions.question: A company is launching a project to build a new customer portal. The sponsor has provided a verbal go-ahead and an approximate budget but has not formally chartered the project. The marketing VP insists that social media sharing features be added 'since we are already building a portal.' The IT director says the existing infrastructure cannot support the expected load. What should the PM do first, and why? Identify at least three specific actions.
questions.answer:
The PM should not proceed with execution until a formal charter exists and scope is explicitly agreed. Specific actions:

1. Draft and obtain a signed project charter: this formalizes the sponsor's authorization, names the PM, sets the preliminary scope, identifies key stakeholders, and documents the business case. Without this, any future scope dispute has no reference document and the PM has no authority to resist scope expansion.

2. Conduct stakeholder identification and analysis: build a stakeholder register including the sponsor, marketing VP, IT director, and end customers; map them on a power/interest grid. This reveals that marketing VP is high-power high-interest (manage closely) and will need a specific engagement strategy.

3. Write a preliminary scope statement with explicit inclusions and exclusions: defer social media sharing to phase 2 unless explicitly added to the charter after impact analysis. Gold plating without authorization is a leading cause of project failure.

4. Surface the infrastructure concern as a risk and a constraint: the IT director's input is valuable project intelligence. Add 'infrastructure capacity' to the risk register and possibly trigger an early feasibility spike before committing to a schedule.

5. Establish the change control process from day one: all subsequent scope changes, including marketing's suggestion, go through impact analysis before approval. This creates a professional footing and prevents the project from being shaped by informal pressure.

rubric.key: Identifies the need for a formal charter before execution
Describes stakeholder analysis and engagement planning
Addresses scope discipline (exclusions, change control) and surfaces IT concern as risk

questions.question: Given the scope statement for a website redesign project, decompose it into a Work Breakdown Structure to at least three levels. The scope includes: new visual design, content updates, CMS migration, SEO optimization, deployment, and user training. Apply the 100% rule and 8-80 rule. Provide a WBS dictionary entry for one work package.
questions.answer:
WBS:
1.0 Website Redesign Project
  1.1 Design
    1.1.1 Wireframes
    1.1.2 Visual design mockups
    1.1.3 Design system and component library
  1.2 Content
    1.2.1 Content audit of existing site
    1.2.2 New content drafting
    1.2.3 Content review and editing
    1.2.4 Content migration to new CMS
  1.3 Technical Implementation
    1.3.1 CMS platform selection and setup
    1.3.2 Theme development
    1.3.3 Content type and field configuration
    1.3.4 Integrations (analytics, forms, search)
  1.4 SEO Optimization
    1.4.1 Keyword research and mapping
    1.4.2 On-page SEO implementation
    1.4.3 Redirect mapping from old URLs
    1.4.4 Sitemap and schema markup
  1.5 Deployment
    1.5.1 Staging environment testing
    1.5.2 UAT with stakeholders
    1.5.3 Production deployment
    1.5.4 Post-launch monitoring (first week)
  1.6 Training
    1.6.1 Training material development
    1.6.2 Administrator training sessions
    1.6.3 Content editor training sessions
  1.7 Project Management
    1.7.1 Planning and coordination
    1.7.2 Status reporting
    1.7.3 Closeout activities

The WBS follows the 100% rule: every element of scope is represented and nothing extra. Work packages are sized for the 8-80 rule; for example, 1.3.2 Theme development is estimated at approximately 60 hours, within range.

WBS Dictionary Entry for 1.3.2:
- Code: 1.3.2
- Name: Theme Development
- Description: Develop the CMS theme implementing the approved visual design system, including all page templates, responsive breakpoints, and component styles
- Responsible: Frontend Developer
- Entry criteria: Design system approved (1.1.3 complete), CMS platform configured (1.3.1 complete)
- Exit criteria: All page templates functional in staging, passes accessibility review, approved by design lead
- Effort estimate: 60 hours
- Deliverables: Theme code repository, deployment package, technical documentation

rubric.key: WBS follows 100% rule with all scope elements represented
Decomposition reaches work packages in the 8-80 hour range
WBS dictionary entry is complete with code, description, owner, criteria, and estimate

questions.question: A sponsor tells you at the kickoff meeting: 'I need this done in six months and it must include features A, B, C, D, E, and F. The budget is $500,000. Make it happen.' You privately estimate, based on your experience and analogous projects, that the full scope would take 9 months and $750,000. What is the most professional response, and what techniques would you use to negotiate?
questions.answer:
The most professional response is to present the trade-off explicitly using the triple constraint, offering options rather than simply refusing or capitulating. Capitulating leads to a death march and guaranteed failure; refusing damages the relationship without helping.

Specific actions:

1. Acknowledge the constraints: thank the sponsor for the clarity and confirm understanding of the six-month, $500,000 targets and the full feature list.

2. Validate with data: request time to do a proper estimate using bottom-up WBS-based estimation with the team. Explain that a gut check suggests the full scope may exceed the budget and schedule, but a rigorous estimate will give the sponsor real numbers to work with.

3. Present options using the triple constraint:
   - Option A (full scope): deliver A-F in approximately 9 months for $750K; explain the estimate basis.
   - Option B (fixed schedule and budget): deliver A, B, C plus most of D in 6 months for $500K; features E and F deferred to phase 2. Explain why these features were prioritized.
   - Option C (fixed schedule, scope, additional budget): deliver A-F in 6 months for $900K with additional resources (warning: adding people does not scale linearly, Brooks' law).
   - Option D (fixed scope and budget, flexible schedule): deliver A-F in 9 months for $500K with a lean team.

4. Recommend option B: most projects benefit most from scope flexibility; it aligns with MVP thinking and delivers value earliest. Ask the sponsor which features are essential and which can defer.

5. Document the decision in the charter: whatever the sponsor chooses becomes the binding commitment and reference for future scope discussions.

6. If the sponsor insists on the impossible combination, state clearly and in writing: 'My professional assessment is that this scope cannot be delivered in this timeframe with this budget. If we proceed as stated, we will miss the deadline or the budget or both.' This is not refusing; it is refusing to lie about the estimate.

Techniques used: triple constraint framing, bottom-up estimation, option-based negotiation, written commitment, professional honesty.

rubric.key: Uses triple constraint framing with concrete options
Applies estimation and negotiation techniques rather than capitulating or refusing
Documents the decision and maintains professional honesty

quizzes.topic: Schedule, Cost, and Earned Value Management

questions.question:
Given the following activity network, compute ES, EF, LS, LF, and total float for each activity, and identify the critical path.

Activities and durations (days):
A=4, B=6, C=3, D=5, E=4, F=2, G=7, H=3

Dependencies:
A has no predecessor
B depends on A
C depends on A
D depends on B
E depends on B, C
F depends on D
G depends on D, E
H depends on F, G
questions.answer:
Forward Pass (ES/EF):
A: ES=0, EF=4
B: ES=4, EF=10 (after A)
C: ES=4, EF=7 (after A)
D: ES=10, EF=15 (after B)
E: ES=max(10,7)=10, EF=14 (after B and C)
F: ES=15, EF=17 (after D)
G: ES=max(15,14)=15, EF=22 (after D and E)
H: ES=max(17,22)=22, EF=25 (after F and G)

Project duration: 25 days

Backward Pass (LF/LS): Starting from H with LF=25
H: LF=25, LS=22
G: LF=22, LS=15
F: LF=22, LS=20
E: LF=15, LS=11
D: LF=min(20,15)=15, LS=10
C: LF=11, LS=8
B: LF=min(10,11)=10, LS=4
A: LF=4, LS=0

Total Float (LS-ES):
A: 0 - 0 = 0 (critical)
B: 4 - 4 = 0 (critical)
C: 8 - 4 = 4
D: 10 - 10 = 0 (critical)
E: 11 - 10 = 1 (near critical)
F: 20 - 15 = 5
G: 15 - 15 = 0 (critical)
H: 22 - 22 = 0 (critical)

Critical Path: A -> B -> D -> G -> H (duration 4+6+5+7+3 = 25 days)

Note that E has only 1 day of float, making it near-critical and worth close monitoring.

rubric.key: Correct forward pass with ES and EF for all activities
Correct backward pass with LS and LF for all activities
Correct total float calculations
Correct identification of critical path and project duration

questions.question:
At the end of month 6 of a 12-month project, the following data is reported:
- Budget at Completion (BAC): $2,400,000
- Planned Value (PV) to date: $1,200,000
- Earned Value (EV) to date: $1,020,000
- Actual Cost (AC) to date: $1,100,000

Calculate: (a) CV, SV, CPI, SPI; (b) EAC assuming current cost performance continues; (c) ETC; (d) VAC; (e) TCPI for remaining work to finish on budget. Interpret the results and recommend actions.
questions.answer:
(a) Variances and indices:
CV = EV - AC = 1,020,000 - 1,100,000 = -$80,000 (over budget by $80K)
SV = EV - PV = 1,020,000 - 1,200,000 = -$180,000 (behind schedule by $180K worth of work)
CPI = EV/AC = 1,020,000/1,100,000 = 0.927 (getting 92.7 cents of value per dollar spent)
SPI = EV/PV = 1,020,000/1,200,000 = 0.850 (85% of planned progress achieved)

(b) EAC (assuming current CPI continues):
EAC = BAC/CPI = 2,400,000/0.927 = $2,588,996 (approximately $2,589,000)

(c) ETC = EAC - AC = 2,589,000 - 1,100,000 = $1,489,000 remaining cost

(d) VAC = BAC - EAC = 2,400,000 - 2,589,000 = -$189,000 (projected over budget by approximately $189K)

(e) TCPI (to complete on budget BAC):
TCPI = (BAC - EV)/(BAC - AC) = (2,400,000 - 1,020,000)/(2,400,000 - 1,100,000) = 1,380,000/1,300,000 = 1.062
This means the remaining work must be performed at 1.062 efficiency (getting $1.062 of value per dollar spent) to finish at the original budget. Current CPI is 0.927, so the team must improve from 0.927 to 1.062, a 14.5% improvement in cost performance.

Interpretation:
The project is both behind schedule and over budget, and the gap between current and required performance is significant. History from the Defense Acquisition University suggests CPI tends to stabilize by the 20% complete mark and rarely improves dramatically. At 6 months of a 12-month project (50% elapsed, but only 42.5% earned), the project is showing systemic issues rather than transient problems.

Recommended actions:
1. Root cause analysis: is the problem productivity, scope growth, poor estimation, or rework? The 5 whys or fishbone can help.
2. Scope review with sponsor: identify features that can be deferred to phase 2 to bring the project back within budget.
3. Replanning: develop an honest re-baseline based on current actual performance rather than the original optimistic estimates.
4. Schedule recovery plan: identify critical path activities that can be compressed and estimate the cost.
5. Stakeholder communication: honest status report to sponsor, presenting options rather than hiding the problem. The worst response is to promise recovery without a concrete plan.

rubric.key: Correct CV, SV, CPI, SPI calculations
Correct EAC, ETC, VAC calculations
Correct TCPI calculation and interpretation
Sound interpretation with practical recommended actions

questions.question: A 20-day project has total planned cost of $80,000. At day 10, the team reports 40% of work is complete, and actual costs are $45,000. The sponsor asks: 'Are we on track?' Explain clearly, using EVM, why a naive answer could mislead the sponsor, and give the correct answer with supporting calculations.
questions.answer:
A naive answer might say 'we have spent $45,000 of our $80,000 budget, which is 56% of budget, so we still have 44% left' or 'we are halfway through the schedule and halfway through the budget, so we are on track.' Both are misleading because they ignore how much work has actually been accomplished.

EVM calculation:
- BAC = $80,000
- PV at day 10 = $80,000 * (10/20) = $40,000 (assuming linear planning)
- EV at day 10 = 0.40 * $80,000 = $32,000 (40% of total work complete)
- AC at day 10 = $45,000

CV = EV - AC = 32,000 - 45,000 = -$13,000 (over budget by $13K for the work done)
SV = EV - PV = 32,000 - 40,000 = -$8,000 (behind schedule by $8K of work)
CPI = 32,000/45,000 = 0.711 (getting only 71 cents of value per dollar)
SPI = 32,000/40,000 = 0.80 (80% of planned progress)

EAC = 80,000/0.711 = $112,500 (projected final cost)
VAC = 80,000 - 112,500 = -$32,500 (projected to finish $32,500 over budget, 40% overrun)

Correct answer to sponsor: 'No, we are not on track. At day 10, we should have completed 50% of the work for $40,000 but have completed only 40% for $45,000. At current performance, we project finishing approximately $32,500 over the original $80,000 budget, a 40% overrun. I recommend [specific corrective actions].'

The key insight is that measuring cost alone (we spent 56% of budget) or elapsed time alone (we are halfway through) does not capture earned value. EVM integrates all three perspectives: planned, earned, and actual, and reveals problems that naive tracking hides.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the flaw in naive cost or time tracking
Correct EVM calculations showing CV, SV, CPI, SPI
Correct EAC forecast and clear explanation to sponsor

questions.question:
A project has identified the following risks with their initial probability (1-5 scale) and impact (1-5 scale):
R1: Key architect may leave (P=3, I=5)
R2: Third-party API may be discontinued (P=2, I=4)
R3: Regulatory change (P=2, I=5)
R4: Budget cut (P=4, I=3)
R5: Requirements ambiguity (P=4, I=4)

For each risk, compute the risk score (P x I), categorize it (Low 1-5, Medium 6-12, High 15+), propose an appropriate response strategy (avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept), and identify the response owner.
questions.answer:
R1: Key architect may leave
Score: 3 x 5 = 15 (High)
Strategy: Mitigate. Reduce probability by engagement (retention conversation, challenging work, recognition); reduce impact by ensuring knowledge redundancy (pair programming, design documentation, architecture review sessions with the team).
Owner: Engineering manager (for retention) and technical lead (for knowledge sharing).

R2: Third-party API discontinuation
Score: 2 x 4 = 8 (Medium)
Strategy: Mitigate with fallback. Reduce impact by designing an adapter layer that isolates our code from the API so an alternative could be substituted. Monitor vendor announcements for early warning.
Owner: Lead developer.

R3: Regulatory change
Score: 2 x 5 = 10 (Medium)
Strategy: Transfer and mitigate. Transfer part of the risk by engaging regulatory counsel; mitigate by building modularity to absorb likely change areas. Monitor regulatory signals.
Owner: Compliance officer; project sponsor informed.

R4: Budget cut
Score: 4 x 3 = 12 (Medium)
Strategy: Mitigate by early delivery of highest-value features (MVP first) so that partial completion still delivers value; escalate to sponsor with business case showing ROI.
Owner: Project manager and sponsor.

R5: Requirements ambiguity
Score: 4 x 4 = 16 (High)
Strategy: Mitigate through early prototyping and structured elicitation. Spend 2 weeks building a clickable prototype and validating with stakeholders before committing to the schedule. Use user stories with acceptance criteria. Adopt an adaptive approach for ambiguous areas.
Owner: Project manager and business analyst.

Highest priority for attention: R5 (16) and R1 (15). Both should have detailed response plans and be monitored closely with explicit triggers.

rubric.key: Correct risk scores and categorization
Appropriate response strategies with reasoning
Response owners correctly assigned by role and authority

questions.question: Your project team is in the storming stage. Two senior engineers publicly disagreed about the architecture in the last meeting, voices were raised, and other team members are disengaging. A junior engineer tells you privately that the conflict feels personal. What is the appropriate response? Use Tuckman's model, psychological safety concepts, and a conflict management framework to justify your answer.
questions.answer:
Context: Tuckman's model teaches that storming is a normal and necessary stage of team formation where conflicts about roles, authority, and approach surface. The PM's job is not to suppress the conflict but to facilitate its productive resolution. Suppression stalls the team in forming; escalation damages relationships. The goal is to move toward norming.

Action plan:

1. Address it quickly and privately, not publicly. Schedule separate one-on-one conversations with each engineer within 24-48 hours. Public confrontation embarrasses and entrenches positions.

2. In each conversation, use active listening. Ask each to describe the disagreement from their perspective. Listen for facts (architecture substance), feelings (frustration, feeling dismissed), and identity (am I respected as an expert). Stone, Patton, and Heen's framework identifies all three layers.

3. Separate the substantive architectural disagreement from the interpersonal friction. Both matter but require different interventions.

4. Facilitate a structured discussion of the architectural question in a later team meeting, using collaborating mode from Thomas-Kilmann: ground rules (attack ideas, not people), present options objectively, evaluate against explicit criteria, decide collectively. This models psychological safety for the whole team.

5. Protect psychological safety: other team members are watching how this is handled. Make it clear that disagreement on substance is welcome; personal attacks are not. Reference Google's research that psychological safety is the #1 predictor of high-performing teams.

6. Follow up on outcomes. After the architectural decision is made, check back with both engineers and the junior engineer to ensure the conflict is resolved at all three layers (facts, feelings, identity).

7. Use the retrospective (if agile) or a team norms conversation (if not) to establish how the team will handle disagreements productively going forward, shifting the team from storming to norming.

What to avoid:
- Taking sides privately or publicly; this destroys neutrality and trust.
- Ignoring the conflict and hoping it resolves; it will not, and disengaged team members signal that the situation is already damaging productivity.
- Reassigning one of the engineers; this avoids learning and punishes productive disagreement.
- Treating it as 'just personalities'; the architectural question is real and must be resolved.

The mature PM treats this as an opportunity: productive conflict resolution builds team strength, while avoidance destroys it.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies storming as normal and addresses it appropriately
Applies conflict management framework (private first, active listening, structured discussion)
Addresses psychological safety and team norm establishment

questions.question: Explain the cost of quality concept. A project has the following quality cost data for the first year: prevention $20K, appraisal $30K, internal failure $90K, external failure $180K (warranty claims and customer complaints). Diagnose the quality health of this project and recommend how to rebalance investment. Total quality cost is $320K.
questions.answer:
Cost of quality concept:
Quality costs fall into four categories. Prevention costs (training, design reviews, process improvements) and appraisal costs (inspection, testing) are costs of conformance, spent to prevent defects or to catch them internally. Internal failure costs (rework, scrap, defects caught before delivery) and external failure costs (customer complaints, warranty, reputation damage) are costs of nonconformance, incurred because quality was not achieved.

The 1-10-100 rule states that a defect costs approximately $1 to prevent, $10 to fix internally, and $100 to fix externally. Therefore, shifting investment from reactive (failure) to proactive (prevention) dramatically reduces total quality cost over time.

Diagnosis of this project:

Current distribution:
- Prevention: $20K (6.25% of total)
- Appraisal: $30K (9.4% of total)
- Internal failure: $90K (28.1% of total)
- External failure: $180K (56.25% of total)

Conformance costs: $50K (15.6%)
Nonconformance costs: $270K (84.4%)

This is a classic sign of a quality-troubled project. Most of the money is being spent reacting to problems after they occur, particularly external failures that are the most expensive type. The high external failure cost suggests the team is letting defects escape to customers, which destroys trust and generates warranty and support costs.

Recommendations:

1. Increase prevention investment from $20K to $60K (training, design reviews, better requirements elicitation, test-driven development adoption, code review culture). The 1-10-100 rule predicts that each dollar in prevention saves approximately $10 in failure costs.

2. Increase appraisal from $30K to $60K (automated testing infrastructure, code coverage tools, earlier testing cycles, dedicated QA resources). Catch defects internally rather than letting them reach customers.

3. Root-cause analysis of the top 5 external failure types using Pareto and fishbone techniques. Address the underlying causes, not just the symptoms.

4. Target for year 2: prevention $60K + appraisal $60K + internal failure $40K + external failure $40K = $200K total. This is a 37.5% reduction in total quality cost by shifting investment forward.

5. Monitor the quality cost metrics quarterly to verify the investment is producing expected savings.

The key insight: cutting total quality costs by cutting prevention and appraisal is the wrong direction. The path to lower total cost is higher prevention investment that eliminates the more expensive failure categories. This is counterintuitive but repeatedly demonstrated in quality literature (Juran, Crosby, Deming).

rubric.key: Correctly explains cost of quality concept and 1-10-100 rule
Accurate diagnosis showing nonconformance dominance
Practical rebalancing recommendations with expected impact

questions.question: Compare firm-fixed-price (FFP) and cost-plus-fixed-fee (CPFF) contracts for a software development engagement. For each, describe: (a) how cost risk is allocated, (b) how the seller is incentivized, (c) what type of scope situation favors it, (d) what management challenges the buyer must address.
questions.answer:
Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP):

(a) Cost risk allocation: The seller bears all cost risk. If the seller's actual cost exceeds the contracted price, the seller absorbs the loss; if actual cost is lower, the seller keeps the difference as additional profit. The buyer knows exactly what they will pay regardless of seller inefficiency or overruns.

(b) Seller incentives: Strong incentive to control costs and deliver efficiently. Negative incentive to add unnecessary work or features. Potential perverse incentive to cut corners on quality or scope if financial pressure grows late in the contract. Seller will typically pad the bid to absorb their own risk (common padding factor: 20-40%).

(c) Scope situation favoring FFP: Well-defined scope with clear acceptance criteria. Mature technology and processes. Experienced seller who can accurately estimate the work. Example: building a standard web application with known tech stack, defined features, and industry-standard patterns.

(d) Buyer management challenges: The buyer must invest heavily in scope definition and change control. Any scope change likely triggers a change order with additional cost. The buyer must monitor quality closely to prevent corner-cutting; acceptance criteria must be precise and testable. Scope creep risks are huge if not controlled, since every addition becomes a change order.

Cost-Plus-Fixed-Fee (CPFF):

(a) Cost risk allocation: The buyer bears cost risk. Actual costs are reimbursed plus a fixed fee (the seller's profit). If actual cost doubles, the buyer pays the doubled cost; the fee remains fixed as originally agreed.

(b) Seller incentives: Weak incentive to control costs because every dollar of cost is reimbursed. Some ethical sellers will be diligent out of reputation concerns, but the contract itself does not reward efficiency. The fixed fee does not grow with cost (unlike cost-plus-percentage-of-cost, which is now illegal in US government contracting for this reason).

(c) Scope situation favoring CPFF: Uncertain scope, research and development work, or early-stage exploration where requirements evolve. Example: developing a new AI algorithm where the approach is not yet known. In such cases, FFP bids would be either refused by sellers or priced so high to absorb risk that the buyer loses the advantage.

(d) Buyer management challenges: Active cost oversight is essential. The buyer must audit seller invoices, require detailed cost reporting, limit labor categories and billable hours, and monitor progress against budget. Earned value management is particularly important. The buyer must also be prepared for the possibility that total cost significantly exceeds initial estimates. The relationship requires strong trust and professional ethics from the seller.

Comparison summary: FFP pushes risk to the seller; CPFF keeps risk with the buyer. Neither is universally better; the right choice depends on how well scope is understood and how much uncertainty exists. Mature procurement often uses hybrid structures (fixed-price incentive fee, cost plus incentive fee) that share risk and align incentives.

rubric.key: Correct analysis of risk allocation and incentives for both types
Appropriate scope situations identified
Practical management challenges for each contract type

questions.question: A Scrum team has a velocity of 30 story points per sprint over the last 5 sprints. The product backlog contains 400 remaining story points. The product owner asks: 'When will we finish everything in the backlog?' and the sponsor asks: 'Can you commit to finishing in 10 sprints?' Provide professional answers to both, and explain the difference between velocity as a planning tool and velocity as a commitment.
questions.answer:
Answer to the product owner:

At current velocity of 30 points per sprint with 400 points remaining, expected completion is approximately 400/30 = 13.33 sprints, or roughly 14 sprints to complete all items. However, this is a projection based on current data, not a commitment, and several assumptions affect the answer:

1. The backlog is likely to grow as new items are discovered or requested (typical growth: 10-25%). With 15% growth, the actual work may be 460 points, requiring approximately 16 sprints.

2. Velocity has variability. Looking at the last 5 sprints, if the range is 25-35 points, the 90% confidence range is wider than the point estimate. A more honest answer is 'between 13 and 18 sprints' rather than a single number.

3. Priorities may change. In agile, the product owner reorders the backlog as they learn; the absolute finish date is less important than the earliest delivery of the highest-value items.

Recommended communication: 'At current velocity, we project roughly 14 sprints to complete the current backlog. If we account for typical backlog growth and velocity variation, the range is 14 to 18 sprints. However, I recommend focusing on when the top 50% of value will be delivered rather than when 100% will be delivered, since that is the more useful business question.'

Answer to the sponsor:

'I cannot commit to a fixed date for an open backlog. I can commit to specific outcomes for specific fixed dates, using one of these patterns:

1. Fixed scope, variable date: if you fix the 400 points as our commitment, I can project (with uncertainty) that this will take 14-18 sprints. But fixing scope in agile defeats the purpose.

2. Fixed date, variable scope: if 10 sprints is the fixed commitment, I can commit to delivering the highest-value 300 points (10 sprints at velocity 30) as of that date. We will work with the product owner to ensure those 300 points are the most valuable features.

3. Release milestones: we can identify logical release points (e.g., MVP after 6 sprints, beta after 10, full release after 14) and communicate progress toward each.

I recommend option 2: commit to a value-oriented outcome (the most important 300 points delivered in 10 sprints) rather than an all-or-nothing commitment to 400 points in 10 sprints that would require working at 133% of current velocity. The latter is not credible based on 5 sprints of data.'

Velocity as planning tool vs commitment:

Velocity is a planning tool. It provides historical data for projection with uncertainty. It is an observed characteristic of this team on this work with this definition of done. Treating velocity as a commitment creates perverse incentives: teams will game velocity by inflating story points, cutting quality corners, or refusing challenging work. Velocity is also subject to external factors (sick days, meetings, interruptions) that a team cannot fully control.

Commitments are made to specific outcomes within fixed constraints, not to velocity. 'We will deliver stories A, B, C by the end of this sprint' is a commitment; 'we will achieve velocity of 35 next sprint' is not a commitment but a target. The distinction protects both the team from unrealistic pressure and the organization from empty promises.

rubric.key: Sound projection for product owner with uncertainty acknowledgment
Value-oriented response to sponsor with clear trade-off options
Correct explanation of velocity as planning tool vs commitment

questions.question: You must deliver a weekly status report to a board of directors. The project is in trouble: it is 3 weeks behind schedule, $50K over budget, and has three open high-severity risks. The sponsor has privately asked you to 'soften the message' to avoid embarrassment. How do you proceed?
questions.answer:
This is primarily an ethics question with practical communication elements.

The PMI Code of Ethics requires honesty in communication. Softening bad news to the point of misleading the board violates honesty and responsibility standards. It also damages the PM's professional reputation and potentially exposes the organization (and the PM personally) to legal liability if material misrepresentations influence board decisions.

Step 1: Have a direct conversation with the sponsor.

'I understand the concern about how this will be received, and I share it. I also have a professional obligation to report status accurately. If I soften the message enough to hide the problems, I risk the board approving decisions based on inaccurate information, and I risk my professional integrity. Let me suggest an alternative: present the honest status paired with a credible recovery plan.'

This reframes the conversation from 'hide the problem' to 'present the problem constructively.' Most sponsors, when given this choice, will accept the honest path, especially if the framing includes a plan.

Step 2: Draft the status report using the pyramid principle.

Top line: 'The project is currently AMBER: 3 weeks behind schedule and $50K over budget, with three open high-severity risks. We have developed a recovery plan that brings the project back within 1 week and $20K of baseline if approved this week.'

Supporting section 1 - Status with data: schedule critical path delayed three weeks because of [specific cause]; cost CPI 0.92; current forecast at completion $X over $Y baseline; risks R1, R2, R3 with descriptions, probability, impact, and current response.

Supporting section 2 - Root causes (not blame, just analysis): specific event or condition leading to each problem; why it was not prevented (for learning, not guilt).

Supporting section 3 - Recovery plan: specific actions with owners and dates; required decisions from the board; expected outcome with residual risk.

Supporting section 4 - Decisions needed from the board this meeting: approve scope adjustment (move feature X to phase 2); approve reallocation of $15K from contingency reserve to recovery effort; confirm endorsement of the revised timeline.

Step 3: Present professionally. Lead with the top line. Do not bury the bad news at the end. Answer questions directly without defensiveness. Own the problems without excuses. Present the recovery plan with confidence based on specifics, not optimism. Acknowledge the sponsor's continued support.

Step 4: Follow up. If the board approves the recovery plan, execute and report progress weekly. If they ask for a different response, adapt. If they question the PM's judgment, that is a signal to have a direct conversation with the sponsor about continued PM assignment.

What not to do: agree to soften the message, present half-truths, omit the risks, blame the team, or accept the sponsor's framing without pushback. Each creates larger problems later.

The principle: integrity at the moment of pressure defines professional character. A PM who reports honestly under difficulty builds a reputation that pays back over a career. A PM who softens and hides builds the opposite reputation and eventually cannot be trusted with difficult projects.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the ethics issue and refuses to mislead
Practical conversation with sponsor offering constructive alternative
Well-structured status report with clear recovery plan and specific decisions needed

questions.question: A project is technically complete: all deliverables have been produced and tested. Describe the essential steps for formal project closure and explain why each matters. Identify at least six specific activities.
questions.answer:
Formal project closure activities:

1. Formal acceptance of deliverables by customer/sponsor (signed sign-off document)
Why: Creates a clear legal and administrative record that scope has been delivered. Without this, disputes can arise months or years later about what was or was not delivered. The signed acceptance is the reference document.

2. Administrative closure of contracts and procurement
Why: Ensures all vendor obligations are met, all deliverables received, all invoices settled, all claims resolved. Open contracts are ongoing liabilities that must be formally closed to eliminate unclear obligations.

3. Final financial reconciliation and budget closeout
Why: Confirms final actual cost against baseline, closes the project budget in the financial system, releases encumbered funds, and provides actual data for future estimates. Without this, the finance team cannot close their books for the project.

4. Transition of deliverables to operations
Why: The project produced something that will be used; someone else must take ownership. Operations must be trained, documentation must be handed over, support processes must be established, access credentials transferred. A sloppy transition generates months of confusion and problem tickets that blame the project.

5. Lessons learned session and knowledge capture
Why: Converts project experience into organizational knowledge for future projects. Without a deliberate capture process, learning stays with individual team members and is lost as they move on. The lessons learned register should be actionable, specific, and searchable.

6. Team release and recognition
Why: Team members return to functional managers or next assignments. Formal release clarifies that their project obligations are complete. Recognition, whether a team celebration, performance write-up, or manager acknowledgment, sustains morale and encourages willing participation in future projects.

7. Project documentation archiving
Why: Future projects, audits, regulatory inquiries, and legal matters may need the project's records. Structured archiving with an index ensures records can be found. Without this, the organization has no institutional memory of the project.

8. Post-implementation review (often 3-6 months after closure)
Why: Confirms whether the project actually delivered the expected business benefits. Many projects deliver outputs on time but fail to deliver outcomes. The post-implementation review closes this loop and provides data for future benefit estimates.

All of these activities are part of administrative closure and collectively ensure that closing is not just stopping the work but properly ending the project. Projects that skip closing activities continue to generate phantom problems for years; projects that close rigorously give the organization a clean slate for the next initiative.

rubric.key: Identifies at least six specific closure activities
Explains the purpose of each activity correctly
Discusses why closing rigorously matters (consequences of skipping)

questions.question: Analyze the Denver International Airport baggage system failure using the PMBOK framework. Identify at least four specific project management failures and explain what should have been done differently.
questions.answer:
Background: The Denver International Airport baggage handling system (1990-1995) was intended to be the most advanced automated baggage system in the world. It failed catastrophically, delaying the airport's opening by 16 months and generating over $560 million in overruns. Luggage was destroyed, misrouted, or lost; the system was eventually abandoned for conventional handling in 2005.

Project Management Failures:

1. Risk Management Failure: The project assumed that an untested technology would work at unprecedented scale. No precedent existed for a system this complex; similar smaller systems had struggled at one-tenth the scale. A proper risk identification process would have flagged 'technology scaling risk' as high probability and catastrophic impact, triggering a risk response plan including a fallback to conventional baggage handling. This risk was either not identified, not taken seriously, or not communicated to decision makers.

What should have been done: Formal quantitative risk analysis early in planning, with explicit probability and impact ratings. Identify the failure of the baggage system as a critical risk to the entire airport opening, not just one subsystem. Develop a fallback plan (conventional handling as Plan B) from the beginning, not as a desperate response after the primary system failed.

2. Scope Management Failure: The scope of the baggage system kept growing as new airlines and terminals were added, often mid-project. Changes were not managed through formal change control, and each addition compounded the technical complexity. The final scope was vastly beyond what was initially feasible.

What should have been done: Rigorous scope baseline with formal change control. Each proposed addition should have been evaluated for impact on schedule, cost, and technical risk before acceptance. Some additions should have been deferred or rejected. The original vendor's ability to scale should have been validated before committing to the expanded scope.

3. Stakeholder Management Failure: The baggage system was driven by the city government but had to serve many airlines, each with different requirements. Airlines were not adequately engaged in early design decisions, leading to late-breaking requirements. The sponsor (city) did not maintain alignment among stakeholders with conflicting interests.

What should have been done: Early and continuous stakeholder engagement with all affected parties. Airlines should have been consulted on requirements and signed off on the design before construction began. Conflicts between stakeholder groups should have been surfaced and resolved at the governance level.

4. Schedule Management Failure: The baggage system was on the critical path for airport opening, but the schedule was determined by the airport opening date rather than by what the baggage system actually required. This forced compressed timelines, concurrent work on designs and construction, and insufficient testing time.

What should have been done: Honest assessment of time required for design, implementation, and testing. If the timeline could not support the technology, either the timeline or the technology should have been changed. The schedule fallacy, that fixing a deadline will force the work to fit, is a well-documented failure mode.

5. Procurement and Contract Failure: The original vendor was selected based partly on their willingness to commit to an aggressive schedule. Contract terms did not adequately address the risk of technology failure or provide for systematic testing and acceptance criteria. When problems emerged, contract disputes complicated recovery.

What should have been done: Vendor selection should have weighted proven capability heavily, not just willingness to meet deadlines. Contract terms should have specified incremental testing and acceptance milestones with the right to pivot approach if early milestones were not met.

6. Governance and Escalation Failure: As problems mounted, the project did not escalate effectively to executive decision-making. The problems were known at working levels long before they became public, but decisions to abandon, replace, or redesign the system were not made until the situation was catastrophic.

What should have been done: Regular executive reviews with honest status reporting. Stage gate reviews where continuation is actively evaluated, including the option to stop or pivot. A culture that rewards early surfacing of bad news rather than punishing the messenger.

Lesson: The Denver baggage system is a textbook case of how multiple project management failures compound into catastrophe. No single failure caused the outcome; the accumulation of risk, scope, schedule, stakeholder, procurement, and governance failures created a situation that could not be recovered. Good project management would have intercepted this failure at multiple points.

rubric.key: Identifies at least four specific PM failures from the case
Correctly applies PMBOK concepts (risk, scope, stakeholder, schedule, procurement, governance)
Describes specific alternatives that could have prevented the failures

questions.question: A vendor with whom your project has a large ongoing contract offers you an expensive gift (a weekend at a resort) in recognition of a smooth-running relationship. Organizational policy is silent on gifts, and your supervisor has not expressed an opinion. What should you do and why?
questions.answer:
The appropriate action is to decline the gift politely, and to do so in a way that preserves the professional relationship without creating awkwardness.

Reasoning:

1. PMI Code of Ethics: The Fairness and Honesty values require avoiding conflicts of interest and the appearance of impropriety. An expensive gift from a vendor with whom we have an ongoing contract creates at least the appearance of impropriety: future decisions about the vendor (performance evaluations, contract renewals, scope changes) could be seen as biased by the gift. This damages the vendor selection process and the PM's professional credibility, regardless of whether actual bias exists.

2. Appearance matters as much as reality: Even if I am certain I will not be biased, an observer has no way to know my internal state. A future decision that favors the vendor will be viewed with suspicion; a decision against them will be viewed as overcompensating. Either way, the gift acceptance has tainted the decision-making environment. Preserving a clean appearance protects both the organization and the PM.

3. Organizational silence is not permission: The absence of a specific policy is not a license to accept. Most organizations have general ethics expectations, and the PM is expected to use judgment in ambiguous cases. The test is: would I be comfortable if this acceptance were front-page news tomorrow or raised in a board meeting? If the answer is no, decline.

4. The gift is disproportionate to professional hospitality norms: A resort weekend is far beyond the token of appreciation category (a branded pen, a working lunch). Large gifts are the category specifically designed to create obligations. Accepting normalizes a practice that could lead to more consequential favors later.

How to decline:

1. Respond promptly and in writing if possible (email), so there is a record. Verbal decline followed by written confirmation is fine.

2. Thank the vendor sincerely for the thought and the good working relationship. The decline is about the gift, not about the relationship.

3. Explain the reason professionally: 'Our organization's ethics guidelines preclude accepting gifts of this type from current vendors, but I very much appreciate the gesture and the partnership that inspired it.' Even if the guideline is not formally written, you are acting consistently with professional ethics.

4. Offer a professional alternative: 'I would welcome the opportunity to discuss our work together over a working lunch.' This keeps the relationship warm and redirects to appropriate hospitality.

5. Inform the supervisor after the fact: brief written note to the supervisor documenting the offer and the decline. This protects the PM from later accusations, creates organizational awareness of vendor practices, and may prompt policy clarification.

Additional considerations:

- If the vendor makes gifts or similar offers to other team members, this is a pattern that warrants broader discussion with leadership.
- If there is a longstanding personal friendship predating the business relationship, disclose it and manage through recusal from contract decisions.
- In some cultures and industries, gift practices differ; consult locally-informed peers, but always err toward avoiding appearance of impropriety.

What to avoid:
- Accepting and 'not letting it influence' decisions: this is how ethical erosion begins.
- Accepting and splitting with team members: this compounds the problem rather than solving it.
- Accepting and feeling resentful that the situation exists: decline cleanly and move on.
- Ignoring the offer without response: ambiguous silence may be interpreted as acceptance.

The principle: professional ethics is tested in small decisions. A PM who declines cleanly in this situation has practiced the habit for larger tests that will come later in a career. The habit of integrity is built by consistent small decisions, not by dramatic large ones.

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Project Plan: Community Health Clinic Construction
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete project management plan for a simulated 18-month, $8.5 million community health clinic construction project. This assignment exercises the full set of planning processes covered in weeks 1-6: charter, stakeholder analysis, scope and WBS, schedule with CPM, cost baseline with EVM preparation, risk register, and quality plan. The project is based on a realistic scenario with genuine stakeholder complexity and technical constraints.
objectives: Apply the full project initiation and planning workflow from charter through baseline approval
Produce integrated planning documents that are internally consistent and traceable to one another
Analyze stakeholder, scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk dimensions for a realistic case
Demonstrate the analytical rigor required of a professional project manager
deliverables: Project charter (2-3 pages) with purpose, objectives, high-level scope, stakeholders, sponsor authority, PM authority, budget summary, and high-level milestones
Stakeholder register and power-interest grid for at least 15 identified stakeholders, with engagement strategies tailored by quadrant
Scope statement and WBS decomposed to work packages (8-80 hour rule), with WBS dictionary entries for at least 10 work packages
Activity list derived from WBS with estimated durations (three-point estimates for at least 10 activities), dependency network, and CPM analysis including critical path identification
Cost baseline as time-phased budget (S-curve) with contingency reserve and management reserve allocations
Risk register with at least 20 identified risks, qualitative analysis, response strategies, and owners
Quality management plan with applicable standards, quality metrics, and quality control activities
Integrated project management plan (15-20 pages) combining all above subsidiary plans with executive summary

criteria: WBS follows 100% rule and 8-80 rule with proper deliverable orientation
CPM calculations are correct with accurate forward and backward passes
EVM preparation data is complete (PV time-phased, BAC identified)
Risk register uses proper probability-impact scoring and response strategies
WBS diagram, network diagram, S-curve, and power-interest grid are professionally formatted
Documents follow consistent formatting and professional presentation
Diagrams clearly communicate structure and relationships
Executive summary concisely communicates key plan elements
All required planning artifacts are present and internally consistent
Traceability from charter through stakeholder analysis to WBS to schedule to budget
Stakeholder engagement strategies are specific and actionable
Risk responses are practical and address identified probability-impact combinations
Professional writing quality throughout
Assumptions are documented explicitly
Design decisions are justified with reasoning
Integration across subsidiary plans is explicit and traceable
Complete integrated project management plan for a realistic construction scenario
Technically correct CPM, EVM setup, and risk analysis
Professional-quality documents with clear visuals and traceability
Comprehensive planning across all knowledge areas

major_assignments.title: Earned Value Analysis and Corrective Action Case Study
major_assignments.description: Given a project scenario with multiple status reporting points, perform complete earned value analysis at each reporting period, track trends, forecast outcomes, and develop evidence-based corrective action plans. This assignment bridges theory and practice, forcing rigorous quantitative analysis and practical management judgment.
objectives: Perform rigorous EVM calculations (PV, EV, AC, CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI) at multiple reporting periods
Interpret EVM results to identify performance issues and their root causes
Develop corrective action plans with specific, measurable recommendations
Communicate EVM analysis in an executive-appropriate format
deliverables: EVM analysis spreadsheet with calculations for 6 monthly reporting periods of a 12-month project, including all standard EVM metrics
Trend analysis charts showing CPI, SPI, and cumulative costs over time
Root cause analysis document (3 pages) diagnosing the performance issues using 5 whys and fishbone techniques
Corrective action plan (4 pages) with specific recommendations, expected impact, and implementation timeline
Executive status report (2 pages) using the pyramid principle, suitable for a board of directors
Recovery scenario forecast showing projected outcomes if corrective actions are implemented vs if not
Written reflection (2 pages) on the decision-making process and alternative approaches considered

criteria: All EVM calculations are mathematically correct for all reporting periods
Trend charts are properly constructed with clear axes and labels
Forecast calculations use appropriate EAC formulas for the scenario
Scenario analysis uses sound assumptions and methodology
Charts and graphs are publication quality with clear legends

Trade-offs and alternatives are explicitly considered
Recommendations are realistic and supported by the analysis
Executive report applies pyramid principle effectively
Technical analysis is translated appropriately for non-technical audience
Writing is professional and precise
Reflection demonstrates genuine insight into PM decision-making
Correct EVM analysis across multiple reporting periods with accurate forecasting
Rigorous root cause analysis and evidence-based corrective actions

Demonstrated analytical rigor and practical management judgment

major_assignments.title: Tailoring PM Practice: Agile, Waterfall, or Hybrid Project Design
major_assignments.description: Given three distinct project scenarios (a regulated medical device development, a marketing website redesign, and a data platform migration), design the appropriate project management approach for each, justifying the choice of predictive, adaptive, or hybrid lifecycle and tailoring the PM practices to fit each context. Then implement one of the three as a detailed plan with the selected approach.
objectives: Analyze project context (scope certainty, regulatory environment, team, stakeholders) to inform approach selection
Justify the choice of predictive, adaptive, or hybrid lifecycle with explicit reasoning
Tailor specific PM practices (planning depth, change control rigor, reporting cadence) to fit the chosen approach
Produce a detailed implementation plan for one of the three scenarios
deliverables: Comparative analysis document (5-7 pages) evaluating the three scenarios on context factors and recommending an approach for each
Tailoring rationale document (3-4 pages) explaining which PM practices to emphasize, simplify, or omit for each scenario
Detailed project management plan for one selected scenario, 12-15 pages, fully tailored to the chosen approach

For the selected scenario: if adaptive, include product backlog, release plan, sprint plan for the first 3 sprints, and definition of done; if predictive, include full WBS, network diagram, and cost baseline; if hybrid, document which elements use which approach and why
Comparison table showing how key PM practices differ across the three scenarios
Executive summary (1 page) for a CIO audience explaining the decision framework used
Presentation slides (12-15 slides) walking through the analysis and recommendations

criteria: Context analysis is thorough and identifies the factors that drive approach selection
Approach recommendations are appropriate for each scenario
Justification explicitly references context factors, not generic advocacy

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for in-class workshops and simulation sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade. PMP-style case discussions require presence to benefit fully.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments submitted after the deadline lose 10% per day, up to a maximum of 5 days. Submissions more than 5 days late are not accepted without prior arrangement. Quizzes cannot be made up without documented excuse.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts and review of each other's logic is encouraged. All quantitative work (WBS, CPM, EVM, risk register) and written analysis must be individual work. Team project deliverables are clearly identified as team submissions.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be original and properly attributed. Using solutions from prior semesters, online solution manuals, or generative AI tools without disclosure constitutes plagiarism. Working through PMBOK concepts with the PMI-published study guides is encouraged and need not be cited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to a scheduling tool (MS Project, GanttPRO, or equivalent), spreadsheet software for EVM calculations, diagramming software (draw.io, Lucidchart, or equivalent), and a PMBOK Guide 7th edition (library copies available, PMI student membership recommended for personal copy). PMP certification preparation resources are encouraged but not required.
